coredata.dc:description: Since its discovery in 2004, graphene and its derivatives have received extensive interest due to their exceptional electrical, thermal, mechanical and optical properties. Not only graphene but graphene quantum dots (GQDs) also are a major focus of academic as well industrial research due to their tunable bandgap, charge carrier selectivity, quantum confinement and fluorescence properties. Also, GQDs have the competence to bind with a large number of molecules/biomolecules via electrostatic interaction and/or π-π stacking interactions, adequate water solubility, excellent biocompatibility and minimal toxicity. The GQDs wide bandgap and their flexibility to engineer its bandgap over an extensive range and carrier selectivity play a major role in catalysis and energy applications. The GQDs progress for particular applications has been reported in many review articles but a comprehensive review article on GQDs for their vast applications is not reported yet. In this review article, recent progress in GQDs is reviewed to provide a comprehensive understanding of synthesis and their potential applications in a variety of fields including biosensors, bioimaging, drug delivery, antimicrobial, phototherapy, photocatalysis, fuel cell, solar cell, energy storage and harvesting. First, GQDs synthesis strategies were discussed, followed by their excellent properties and recent advances in various applications. In the last section, current and future research directions of GQDs research are also discussed.

entry.italic: Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, B. subtilis and Pseudomonas aeruginosa

row.entry:
MICs of GQDs for E. coli and B. subtilis was observed as ∼512 μg mL−1.
MICs of GQDs for Staphylococcus aureus and Pseudomonas aeruginosa were observed as ∼256 μg mL−1.
GQDs are non-toxic to NIH 3T3 and HEK 293 T cells up to the concentration of ∼200 μg mL−1.

MIC for Pseudomonas aeruginosa is 25 μg mL−1
MIC for Staphylococcus aureus is 50 μg mL−1
FIC index is below 0.5 indicating that there is a synergistic effect between Ag-NPs and GQDs
Cell viability of normal mammalian cells treated with Ag-GQDs showed 100% cell viability revealing its excellent biocompatibility.

Upon UV irradiation for 5 min the % bacterial survival is
85% for GQDs alone
50% for ZnO
0% for ZnO-GQDs
Antimicrobial activity for ZnO-GQDs upon irradiation is ascribed to the formation of reaction oxygen species with minor contribution from the membrane damage.
GOQD coating layer effectively inhibits the growth of the bacterial cells on the PVDF membrane.
GOQDs covalently Functionalized PVDF membrane revealed enhanced bactericidal and antibiofouling performances.
Complete inhibition of the bacterial cell growth is reported on incubation of E. coli cells with N-GQDs upon exposure with 670 nm laser only for 3 min.
Nitrogen-bonding compositions of GQDs is more effective for photodynamic antimicrobial therapy.

entry.italic: Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli and Pseudomonas aeruginosa

row.entry:
MICs of TiO2-GQDs for E. coli, P. aeruginosa, and S. aureus were observed to be ∼64, 128, and 64 μg mL−1.
MICs of TiO2 for E. coli, P. aeruginosa, and S. aureus were observed to be 128, 256, and 64 μg mL−1.
MICs of GQDs for E. coli, P. aeruginosa, and S. aureus were observed to be 256, 512, and 512 μg mL−1.
Owing to superior photocatalytic properties, TiO2-GQDs showed better antimicrobial and antibiofilm properties under UV-light due to enhancement in the formation of reactive oxygen species.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Abbreviations: MIC-minimum inhibitory concentration, FIC- fractional inhibitory concentration.

row.entry: GQDs are generated electrochemically and regenerate reactive oxygen species when photoexcited
It is helpful in the health care sector as it can kill both strains of pathogenic bacteria, namely methicillin-resistant E. coli and S. aureus.
They tend to produce singlet oxygen.
Ultra-small size, good biocompatibility, and intrinsic stability fluorescence, near-infrared response character
It can be used as a theranostic agent comprised of aptamer AS1411 and GQDs.
Co9S8-based materials for hydrogen and oxygen electro catalysis.
It is a Co-rich component, and the existence of a Pt-like crystal plane has unique properties.
The practical applications of Co9S8 based materials are in renewable energy devices, include water-splitting and metal-air batteries.
Polydopamine stabilized GQD-photosensitizer Nanocomposites (denoted as PC@GCpD(God)).
The PC@GCpD (God) inhibits the efficiency to quench the EMT6 murine mammary cancer completely and thus helps in cancer therapy.
A new PDT agent based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can produce 1O2 via a multistate sensitization process.
These GQDs can be utilized as PDT agents and can allow imaging. It provides highly effective treatment for cancer therapy.
Highly effective Near-infrared (NIR) GQDs helps in deep imaging penetration and low fluorescence background and produces firmly 1O2 for PDT.
It can be used for intracellular tumor-related glutathione (GSH) detection and enhancement of photodynamic therapy by reducing GSH levels in cancer cells.
It has a more extensive and intact sp2 domain along with carboxyl groups. It has better photostability, increased tumor-targeting ability and also enhanced photothermal conversion efficiency.
It is capable of treating cancer cells and effectively eradicates tumors.
These are useful in cancer therapy as cell death could be traced using GQD-labeled MDA-MB-231 cells post-therapy by utilizing the photostability of GQDs.
Incorporating PEG into the NP surface (PEGylation) is highly efficient due to its unique properties.
The addition of PEG to NPs reduces RES uptake and increases circulation time compared to the uncoated counterparts, thereby being useful in both imaging and drug-delivery and applications.
A simple dipeptide- or amphiphilic amino acid-tuned self-assembly of photosensitizers (PSs).
Having therapeutic features, like high loading efficiency, tunable size, on-demand drug release responding to pH, surfactant, and enzyme stimuli, good cellular uptake and biodistribution.
The outcome exhibits the enhancement of PDT efficacy in vitro and in vivo, which leads to the disruption of complete tumors in mice and thus can helps in cancer therapy.
(GQDs) doped with nitrogen and functionalized with an amino group (amino-N-GQDs).
(GQDs) doped with nitrogen and functionalized with an amino group (amino-N-GQDs) serving as a photosensitizer in PDT had the superior ability to generate ROS than unmodified GQDs.
N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) exhibits enhanced electrocatalytic, photochemical, and electrochemical activities, thereby valuable for biomedical and optoelectronic applications.
zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) and embedded GQDs

The monodisperse ZIF-8/GQD nanoparticles with a 50–100 nm particle size encapsulate DOX during the synthesis procedure and trigger DOXrelease under acidic conditions. They can easily convert near-infrared (NIR) irradiation into heat and thus can enhance the temperature.
They help in the treatment of breast cancer 4T1cells as a model cellular system.
GQDs as caps and magnetic Mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MMSN).
The monodisperse MMSN/GQDs nanoparticles of 100 nm generate heat, increasing the temperature in the range of hyperthermia.
The chemo-photo thermal therapy loaded with the DOX- MMSN/GQDs nanosystem exhibits a notable synergistic effect, resulting in a higher efficacy to kill cancer cells.

multifunctional GQD-MSNs
having Mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) capped with GQDs.
They are highly efficient and have better biocompatibility properties, increased drug loading capacity, and convenient control of drug release.
DOX- GQD-MSNs had a synergistic chemo-photo thermal effect and show a higher effect to kill cancer cells and thus helps in drug delivery as Nanocarriers for cancer therapy.
The cGdots vibrates and form electron clouds, thereby generating sufficient heat (>50 °C) and thus can treat cancer cells by thermal ablation.
They are helpful for the treatment of tumors by using the laser. It results in ∼a 70% reduction in the final volume of the tumor compared to the saline-treated tumor.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since its discovery in 2004, graphene and its derivatives have received extensive interest due to their exceptional electrical, thermal, mechanical and optical properties. Not only graphene but graphene quantum dots (GQDs) also are a major focus of academic as well industrial research due to their tunable bandgap, charge carrier selectivity, quantum confinement and fluorescence properties. Also, GQDs have the competence to bind with a large number of molecules/biomolecules via electrostatic interaction and/or π-π stacking interactions, adequate water solubility, excellent biocompatibility and minimal toxicity. The GQDs wide bandgap and their flexibility to engineer its bandgap over an extensive range and carrier selectivity play a major role in catalysis and energy applications. The GQDs progress for particular applications has been reported in many review articles but a comprehensive review article on GQDs for their vast applications is not reported yet. In this review article, recent progress in GQDs is reviewed to provide a comprehensive understanding of synthesis and their potential applications in a variety of fields including biosensors, bioimaging, drug delivery, antimicrobial, phototherapy, photocatalysis, fuel cell, solar cell, energy storage and harvesting. First, GQDs synthesis strategies were discussed, followed by their excellent properties and recent advances in various applications. In the last section, current and future research directions of GQDs research are also discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials like fullerene, diamond, carbon nanofiber, carbon nanotubes, graphene and graphene oxide (GO) have been well studied for various potential applications [1–4]. The discovery of graphene in 2004 has opened a new opportunity for scientists and engineers [5]. Graphene and graphene-based materials have shown excellent properties like high electron mobility, ultrahigh graphene has been known for its excellent charge career mobility, highest thermal conductivity, ultimate mechanical properties and high surface area [6–9]. Graphene's ultrahigh electron mobility makes it the best choice for electronic/optoelectronic device applications. But graphene's zero-bandgap is the main technical problem for its electronic applications. Conventional graphene sheet has a size of few micrometers. Its bandgap can be opened by reducing graphene sheet size and controlled hetero-atom doping. Graphene sheet dimensionality can be reduced from two-dimensional (2D) to zero-dimensional (0D) by reducing its lateral size. In common practice, a graphene sheet of size less than ≤10 nm, considered as 0D material and referred to as a graphene quantum dot (GQDs) [10]. GQDs extraordinary luminescence properties and the presence of functional groups and defects on its surface make it useful for practical applications [11,12].

Considerable attention has been made in GQDs research for various potential applications including energy conversion and storage, catalysis, flexible devices and biomedical applications [13–17]. The GQDs display exceptional properties such as tunable bandgap, charge carrier selectivity, quantum confinement, fluorescence and many more which enable them suitable candidates for a range of applications. The GQDs have been most studied for biomedical applications including drug delivery, bioimaging, photothermal therapy, biosensors, etc. due to their ultrafine lateral size, remarkable electrochemical and optical properties, their competence to bind with a large number of molecules/biomolecules via electrostatic interaction and/or π-π stacking interactions, adequate water solubility, excellent biocompatibility and minimal toxicity. As a drug delivery vehicle, GQDs are very popular in improving the chemotherapeutic efficiency of anticancer drugs like cisplatin or doxorubicin by accelerating their nuclear intake and promoting their DNA cleavage activity [18]. The availability of different reactive groups on the graphene surface allows the multimodal attachment of the drugs, targeting ligands and the desired polymers to advance their pharmacological parameters both in-vitro and in-vivo [19]. Employing the inherent fluorescence properties of the GQDs, drug nanocarriers could be easily visualized in real-time and their cellular uptake and movement can be monitored [20].

The GQDs wide bandgap and flexibility to engineer its bandgap over an extensive range and carrier selectivity are the key characteristics that contribute to enhancing the photoconversion efficiency of a variety of solar cells. Not only the application, but the introduction of GQDs in solar active materials also open a great opportunity to uncover interesting photophysics. This is why “GQDs for solar cells application” is one of the burning topics of current and future research, and the research papers on this subject are routinely appearing in leading journals. Indeed, the application of GQDs has improved the performance of organic photovoltaics (OPVs) [21], dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) [22], perovskite solar cells (PSCs) [23], silicon solar cells (SSCs) [24] and other novel heterojunction solar cells [25]. Also, GQDs have shown promising applications in photocatalysis, energy harvesting and storage applications. Many review articles have been published on the progress of GQDs for a particular application but a comprehensive review article would be useful to highlights the current status and future research directions of GQDs. In this review article, first, we critically reviewed the progress in GQDs synthesis by using various top-down and bottom approaches. Then, GQDs applications in drug delivery, photothermal, solar cell, energy harvesting and energy storage applications are described in detail. In last, a future research direction of GQDs research is proposed. This is a unique comphrensive review article, which includes almost all aspects of GQDs in terms of development in synthesis, properties and remarkable wide range of applications.

The GQDs are being synthesized by both physical and chemical methods. The GQDs synthesis can be categorized into top-down and bottom-up approaches. In the bottom-up approach, GQDs are mainly synthesized using pyrolysis or carbonization of small organic precursors, cage opening of fullerene and template methods. In contrast, GQDs in the top-down approach were produced by sizing the large size graphitic carbon materials. In past, GQDs synthesis has been reviewed by several authors. Here, we report the recent advancement in GQDs synthesis, which has been evaluated for various potential applications.

The first step in top-down methods is the synthesis of GO sheets, followed by conversing into quantum dots in various ways and are named accordingly. Li et al. [26] produced GO quantum dots using microwave irradiation on GO sheets dispersion under acidic conditions (3.2 M HNO3 and 0.9 M H2SO4) for 3 h. Fig. 1 described the cleaving mechanism in GO sheets. The average diameter of produced GQDs was 4.5 nm and showed greenish-yellow luminescent with quantum yield (QYs) of 11.7%. Bright blue luminescent GQDs were produced via the reduction of obtained GQDs. As obtained blue GQDs exhibit high QYs of 22.9%. GQDs synthesis was further improved by a microwave-hydrothermal method. An aqueous solution of GO (50 mg/mL) was mixed with HNO3 (65%) and H2SO4 (98%) and the mixture was sealed into a Teflon microwave reaction vessel followed by a microwave irradiation for 5 min at 200 °C [27]. As produced GQDs were monodispersed of ca. diameter of 3 nm. GQDs were also synthesized via the two-step microwave-hydrothermal method. The diameter of two-step MW-hydrothermal produced GQDs was 2–6 nm [28]. The bulk production of GQDs was also reported directly from graphite flakes by using microwave irradiation [29]. High-powered microwaves (300 and 600 W) were irradiated on natural graphite flakes under acidic and oxidative conditions. The production yield of GQDs was 70% with an average size of 2–5 nm. Further, GQDs were synthesized via acid-free one-step oxidative cleaving of GO by sonochemical and microwave heating for 30 min at heating powers of 100–400 W [30]. The GQDs production yield was obtained in the range of 75–81% with a diameter of 2–5 nm and QYs of 23.8%.

White light-emitting GQDs (WGQDs) were also synthesized using a combination of microwave and ultrasonic-assisted methods [31]. First, yellow-green fluorescent GQDs were synthesized using ultrasonication and microwave irradiation on oxidized graphite. The WGQDs were obtained by microwave irradiation on prepared GQDs for 12 h in an alkaline solution (pH = 13). TEM analysis confirms the lateral size of GQDs in the range of 2–5 nm. The colloidal solution of WGQDs exhibits a broad emission peak at 445 nm. These WGQDs were used for the fabrication of white-light-emitting diode devices. In other work, a microwave-hydrothermal method was used for glucose-derived GQDs synthesis [32]. The average size of as-prepared GQDs was 1.65 nm, which exhibits emission of 4.1 eV and QYs of 7–11%. Recently, microwave-assisted produced GQDs have been tested for the removal of triazine [33].

The GQDs were extensively synthesized using the hydrothermal method. Water-soluble blue-luminescent GQDs of size 5–13 nm were produced from a hydrothermal cutting of oxidized graphene sheets [34]. Fig. 2 showed the proposed mechanism of conversion of graphene sheets into GQDs by using the hydrothermal method and their photoluminescence (PL) mechanism. Fig. 2a left illustrated how the presence of mixed epoxy on graphene sheets makes them fragile and readily attacked. The defects like oxygen-containing functional groups on graphene sheets can further break the cleaved graphene sheets. The proposed scheme also explains structural models of GQDs in acidic and alkali media and PL mechanism. Further, it was noted that blue-luminescent GQDs emit strongly in alkaline media but are almost quenched in acidic media [35]. Optically tunable edge-terminated GQDs were produced from a hydrothermal procedure [36]. The HRTEM analysis exhibits the homogenous GQDs of an average diameter of 2.5 nm. Water dispersed GQDs exhibit bright luminescence. Tian et al. [37] used an eco-friendly oxidant, hydrogen peroxide for GQDs synthesis. In this method, expanded graphite was mixed in DMF, followed by mixing hydrogen peroxide. Then, the mixture was placed into an autoclave and heated at 170 °C up to 5 h. The reaction introduced oxygen and hydroxyl groups on a graphite surface, which act as sizing of graphite. The diameter of GQDs was in the range of 20–40 nm but thickness ranged from 1 to 1.5 nm. The QY was calculated to be 15%. Later, GQDs were synthesized from a one-pot synthesis method by using graphite as a starting material [38]. First, graphite powder was mixed with potassium permanganate and sulfuric acid, followed by the addition of nitric acid. Then, the mixture was placed into an autoclave and heated at different temperatures. Highly luminescent size-tunable GQDs of 50 nm–2.5 nm were produced under different reaction conditions.

The electrochemical process is a well-known technique for the synthesis of graphene and GQDs. In this process, carbon precursors including graphite, carbon nanotubes and carbon nanofibers can be cut into small sizes and simultaneously formed size-tunable GQDs. In general, carbon precursors are used as an anode and dipped into an aqueous or non-aqueous electrolyte under a voltage of 1–15 V. This process can take a long time and also produced a low yield of GQDs. Shinde et al. [39] reported GQDs by electrochemical method. They prepared green luminescent GQDs from MWCNTs as a working electrode in propylene carbonate with LiClO4. First, MWCNTs get oxidized upon applying a potential (1.1 V versus QRE). The oxidation attributed to intercalation of Li+ into MWCNTs and produced size-tunable GQDs upon reduction. The size of the GQDs can be tuned by tuning the electrolyte temperature. As obtained GQDs with uniform sizes of 3, 5 and 8.2 nm diameter exhibits green luminescence with QYs of 6.3%. By using the same electrolyte, propylene carbonate with LiClO4, GQDs were also synthesized from GO at room temperature [40]. The size of the GQDs can be tuned by varying the applied potential, the concentration of electrolyte, oxidation and reduction time. Large-scale GQDs were synthesized by electrochemical tailoring of graphite oxide [41]. The graphite oxide powder was used as a reactant with water as electrolyte and Pt sheets were used as anode and cathode. A constant voltage of 15 V was applied for 3 h, which is enough to oxidize the water and simultaneously generation of hydroxyl and oxygen radicals. These radicals make the GO structure fragile and therefore the formation of GQDs. Li et al. demonstrated GQDs synthesis from graphene sheets by using graphene film as a working electrode and phosphate buffer solution (PBS) as electrolyte [42]. A cyclic voltammetric scan from −3 to +3 ​V in 0.1 ​M PBS was performed to etched out the GQDs. The resulted GQDs were found to be 3–5 ​nm with 3–6 layers. The GQDs exhibits green luminescent and high stability in water for several months. A similar scan voltage, they further reported nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) by replacing PBS electrolyte with acetonitrile containing tetrabutylammonium perchlorate [43]. The resulted N-GQDs exhibits blue luminescence with strong electrocatalytic activity.

Water-soluble highly fluorescent GQDs were synthesized by electrolysis of graphite rod in a 0.1 M NaOH solution [44]. The resulting solution was reduced by hydrazine hydrate at room temperature. As prepared GQDs exhibits strong yellow fluorescence, which may be the presence of hydrazide groups of GQDs surface created during hydrazine hydrate treatment. Similarly, water-soluble red-fluorescent GQDs were prepared from the electrolysis of graphite rod in a 0.01 M K2S2O8 solution at 5 V potential [45]. The study showed interesting results that observation of red fluorescence was specific to K2S2O8 electrolyte, there is no fluorescence when electrolyte changed to K2F204. As obtained GQDs of size 3–5 nm have been demonstrated for stem cell labeling and other biomedical applications. Ahirwar et al. [46] prepared GQDs and GO quantum dots (GOQDs) by using graphite rods as electrodes and citric acid and alkali hydroxide in water as an electrolyte as shown in Fig. 3 . The formation of GQDs was controlled by varying alkali hydroxide concentrations. A balanced reaction of citric acid and alkali hydroxide (1 ​M citric acid ​+ ​3 ​M NaOH) produced GQDs, while other concentrations of NaOH (1.5, 2 and 4 ​M) gives GOQDs. The diameter of GQDs and GOQDs was calculated in the range of 1.5–4.5 ​nm. In recent work, semi-carbazide functionalized N-GQDs were prepared from direct exfoliation and oxidation of graphite rods at an applied potential of 5 ​V [47]. The average diameter of N-GQDs was 20 nm. The GQDs exhibits strong yellow fluorescence, which is due to the presence of nitrogen and oxygen functional groups on the GQDs surface. Peng et al. [48] demonstrated phosphorus and sulfur-doped GQDs by electrolysis of graphite rod in an alkaline solution. As prepared phosphorus and sulfur-doped GQDs have shown strong electrochemical luminescence (ECL) properties for the design of ECL-based immunosensors.

Oxidative cutting is the most widely used method for GQDs preparation. In this approach, GQDs are prepared by cutting the C–C bonds of precursor materials like graphene or CNTs under strong oxidizers. Shen et al. [35] demonstrated facile preparation of GQDs under oxidative cutting process. First, conventional GO sheets were cut into smaller size GO sheets by further oxidation in HNO3. As obtained smaller size GO sheets were treated with an oligomeric PEG diamine, which acts as a surface passivation agent. Then, PEG-treated GO sheets were reduced by hydrazine hydrate. Eventually, obtained surface passivated GQDs exhibit strong fluorescence and up conversion properties. The PL quantum yield was obtained to be 7.4%. Further, size-tunable GQDs were produced from the GO solution [49]. The GO solution was synthesized using modified Hummer's method. Then, H5IO6 was mixed into prepared GO dispersion and the mixture was heated at 60OC for 24 h. The pH of the solution was maintained to neutral by washing and centrifugation. Afterward, sodium polystyrene sulfonate (PSS) was added into aqueous GO dispersion, followed by 2 h sonication. Finally, Finally, l-ascorbic acid (l-AA) was added into GO nanosheet dispersion and continuously stirred for 24 h at 50 °C. after completion of the reaction, GO dispersion color changed from yellow to dark black, which indicates the reduction of GO nanosheets into GQDs. As prepared GQDs exhibits size of 5–15 nm, which were used for heavy metal ions detection.

Not only GO precursors but GQDs were also prepared by using another carbon source such as carbon nanofibers, CNTs and natural carbon sources. Peng et al. [50] reported GQDs preparation via oxidative cutting of traditional pitch-based carbon fibers. Pitched carbon fibers were oxidized into a concentrated mixture of H2SO4 and HNO3 and sonicated for 2 h. The mixture was stirred for 24 h at three different temperatures (80, 100 and 120 °C), cooled and diluted with deionized water. The obtained GQDs solution was purified via dialysis. This method produced GQDs of different sizes ranging from 1 to 4 nm in scalable amounts with zigzag edge structure. As prepared GQDs exhibits size-tunable PL properties. Their dispersibility in water as well as in other polar solvents, stable luminescence, low toxicity and biocompatibility are useful for medical applications. Further, GQDs were prepared by acid treatment and chemical exfoliation of MWCNTs [51]. As synthesized GQDs exhibit 2D morphology with a zigzag structure but uniform size. The study revealed the formation of functional groups on zigzag sites due to acid treatment, which gives pH-dependent fluorescence properties of GQDs. GQDs were also synthesized from the oxidation of carbon black and various types of coal [52,53]. As prepared GQDs have been demonstrated for imaging and other potential applications. In recent work, Melo et al. [54] prepared GOQDs and rGOQDs by oxidative treatment of GO. First, GO was prepared using modified Hummer's method. Then, a known amount of as-prepared GO dispersion was mixed into concentrated H2SO4 and HNO3 (1:3 ratio). The mixture was first stirred for 24 h and then sonicated also for 24 h. The mixture pH 8 was adjusted by NaOH solution and then filtered through the membrane. The average diameter of 17.7 nm was calculated from TEM analysis.

The GQDs can be synthesized by controlled pyrolyzing and carbonization of a suitable precursor including hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene (HBC), polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and citric acid. Liu et al. [55] demonstrated GQDs synthesis by pyrolysis of unsubstituted HBC. The uniform photoluminescent GQDs of ∼60 nm diameter and 2–3 nm thickness were obtained. Dong et al. [56] demonstrated GQDs preparation by carbonization of citric acid as shown in Fig. 4 . The GQDs were obtained by dispersing the carbonized product into alkaline solutions. In this study, tuning the degree of carbonization of citric acid is important. Complete carbonization gives GO nanosheets but incomplete carbonization yields functionalized GQDs. As synthesized GQDs were found in nanostructure form of ∼15 nm size with average height of 1.4 nm (Fig. 4b and c). The GQDs exhibits excitation-independent luminescence properties with strong PL quantum yield of 9.0%. In other work, the GQDs were also synthesized by pyrolysis of l-glutamic acid [57]. TEM analysis showed GQDs average diameter ∼4.66 nm. As prepared GQDs showed strong fluorescence properties under visible light irradiation with a very high quantum yield of 54.5%. Also, the GQDs exhibits fluorescence in the near-infrared range of 800–850 nm. The strong fluorescence properties of GQDs were explored for in vivo and in vitro cell imaging and detection of H2O2. The synthesis of GQDs by carbonization of citric acid is reported by other researchers [58,59] Hong et al. [60] demonstrated monolayer GQDs were synthesis by pyrolysis of trisodium citrate.

In addition to PAHs and HBCs, nitrogenous aromatic compounds were also used for nitrogen-doped GQDs. Zhu et al. [61] reported GQDs preparation by carbonization of melamine powder at 800–1200 °C and 4 GPa for 72 h. TEM analysis confirms the GQDs diameter of 2–6 nm. The main advantage of this method is in situ nitrogen doping in the lattice due to high temperature and pressure conditions. In other work, nitrogen-doped GQDs were prepared by simple thermal pyrolysis of lemon juice [62]. The GQDs were also synthesized by pyrolysis of naturally available biopolymer alginate at 900 °C [63]. Recently, Kashani et al. [64] reported amino-functionalized GQDs via pyrolysis of citric acid as a carbon source and Histidine as a functionalized agent. Amino-functionalized GQDs (His) was found to be ∼2.0 nm diameter with a photoluminescent quantum yield of 62.8%. These His-GQDs were employed for cellular imaging of human cell lines.

In this approach, GQDs are prepared through the controlled synthesis of organic compounds. The method produced uniform GQDs but the procedure is complex and a long time needed for reactions. Li et al. [65] reported size-tunable stable GQDs through the solution chemistry route. The synthesis scheme of GQDs consists of 168, 132 and 170 conjugated carbon atoms, denoted as 1,2 and 3 respectively, as shown in Fig. 5 . The synthesis starts from 3-iodo-4-bromoaniline (denoted as 4) and other substituted benzene derivatives (denoted as by 5 and 6, respectively). Later on, stepwise Suzuki coupling reactions lead to the formation of polyphenylene dendritic precursors for GQDs. The oxidative condensation of polyphenylene dendritic precursors through step-wise solution chemistry, which led to the formation of graphene moieties. The resulted graphene moieties were stabilized by multiple 2′,4′,6′-triakyl phenyl groups through covalent bonding to edges. The GQDs were highly soluble in common organic solvents. The work was further extended for high water solubility [66]. Further, a facile one-pot method was developed for sulfur-doped GQDs from hydrothermal of 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) as a sulfur source and 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) as a carbon source [67]. TEM analysis revealed uniform S-GQDs of ∼2.5 nm and emitted strong fluorescence under UV-light irradiation. These fluorescent properties of S-GQDs were used for Ag+ detection.

Fullerene is a zero-dimensional material having a size of a few nm. This small size of fullerene can form small size sheets upon opening. With this idea, scientists demonstrated GQDs synthesis by cage opening of a fullerene molecule. Li et al. [68] synthesized GQDs by cage opening of fullerene molecule on ruthenium (Ru) surface, which act as a catalyst. The reaction was performed under an ultra-high vacuum at a high temperature. The strong interaction between the C60 molecule and Ru surface created vacancies on the Ru surface on which the fullerene molecule was embedded. At high temperatures and ultrahigh vacuum, fullerene molecules decompose into small carbon clusters. This small carbon cluster formed GQDs by joining together. In another work, GQDs were prepared by cage opening of C60 molecules via chemical oxidation [69]. In this process, fullerene C60 was oxidized by the Hummers' method. Fig. 6 showed the fullerene cage opening and fragmentation process under strong acid and chemical oxidant. Table 1 summaries the synthesis methods of GQDs along with their proposed applications.

The GQDs are nanometer fragments of graphene, typically ranged from 1.5 to 60 nm. They can be single-layer or few layers of graphene having properties of quantum dots. As discussed in section 2, GQDs can be synthesized top-down and bottom-up approach both. The properties of QGDs are highly influenced by their size, shape, presence of functional groups, defects and dopant. Here, we discussed the key properties of GQDs and their role in a particular application.

The optical properties of QGDs are important for a variety of applications including solar cell, bioimaging, photocatalysis, etc. The optical properties of GQDs depend on defects, doping, presence of surface functional groups. GQDs exhibit strong absorption in the UV region and also can be in the visible region. For example, the UV–visible spectrum of GO showed two distinct peaks at ∼230 nm, which arise from π-π٭ transition of aromatic CC bonds and a weak edge at ∼300 nm due to n- π٭ transition of aromatic CO bonds. Due to the quantum confinement effect, GQDs absorption spectrum strongly depends on the size as shown in Fig. 7 [12]. The absorption peak of GQDs is blue-shifted by reducing their size. The inset shows the absorption peak energy as a function of the average diameter of GQDs. As the GQDs size increases from 5 to 35 nm, the peak energy almost monotonically decreases from ∼6.2 to ∼4.6 eV, very close to that of a graphene sheet. Interestingly, the GQDs absorption spectrum varies with its synthesis methods. Different synthesis methods can produce different surface states. For example, hydrothermally synthesize GQDs of diameter 9.6 nm exhibits similar UV absorption (320 nm) peak to solvothermal obtained GQDs of 5 nm diameter [46]. A similar absorption spectrum was obtained from GQDs with an average size of 3 nm and 5.3 nm prepared via a solvothermal route using DMF as solvent [70]. However, GQDs of 3 nm diameter prepared by electrochemical exfoliation of graphite, exhibit a typical π-π٭ absorption at 227 nm [45]. GQDs prepared from carbon fiber at the different reaction temperatures, exhibit blue, green and yellow emissions respectively [50].

PL is one of the key properties of GQDs. As, graphene is a zero band gap material, thus the origin of quantum confinement in GQDs is important. The band gap opening and associated PL behavior are strongly depending on the size of GQDs. Previous studies reported that GQDs can exhibits PL emission in a wide range from UV to red. Kim et al. [12] reported anomalous size dependences on the visible PL, which could due to variation in GQDs shape and edge-state. Fig. 8 shows the common trend in PL emission with the variation in size and morphology of GQDs [71]. The PL properties of GQDs do not only depend on the size and shape of GQDs but also external factors including experimental conditions and microenvironment conditions. Li et al. [72] reported a detailed study on the temperature influence PL properties at different excitation wavelengths. The normalized PL intensity was reduced very significantly (more than 50%) for excitation wavelengths of 310 nm, 340 nm and 365 nm within a temperature range less than 80 °C. The interaction of GQDs with surroundings can play important role in their PL spectra. The PL spectra of GQDs encapsulated in zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF) nanocrystals were found to be red-shifted by 32 nm, which is most likely due to interaction between GQDs and ZIF nanocrystals [73]. In other work, PL spectra was shifted from blue to green when GQDs placed in the vicinity of a single silver nano-octahedron (SNO) [74]. Further, it was also reported that solution pH is capable to tune the fluorescent spectra of GQDs. For example, at pH 7, GQDs exhibits PL emission peak at 480 nm, which shifted to 440 nm at pH 6.7 [75]. Yang et al. also found pH-dependent fluorescence of GQDs of 2–5 nm [76]. With the increase in pH, GQDs fluorescence intensity was found to increase. The possible mechanism of pH-dependent fluorescent properties of GQDs has been reported elsewhere [77,78]. The optical properties of GQDs are important for various applications such as biosensors, bioimaging and photovoltaics. To employ the PL properties of GQDs, not only fluorescence but their quantum yield is equally important. Basically, quantum yield is providing the how much fraction of molecules emitting a photon upon direct excitation by a source.

With these excellent PL properties, GQDs are potential candidates for sensor materials, bio imaging applications, drug delivery systems. Moreover, they are extensively used as fluorescent probes for several analytes because of their substantial properties such as photobleaching resistance, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, stable PL and high aqueous solubility [8]. Furthermore, PL is the most interesting attribute of GQDs that can be easily modified by managing their size, surface functionalization, and chemical doping [9]. Their adjustable and robust PL and confirmed eco-friendly and low toxicity nature make GQDs an attractive candidate for bio-imaging applications. In addition, the surface functionalization phenomenon improved the biocompatibility of GQDs and it is a growing area of research for various applications in the biomedical field [10]. Moreover, GQDs with edge functionalization hold carboxyl, carbonyl, epoxy, and hydroxyl groups which can interconnect with various biological molecules such as proteins, antibodies, enzymes, etc. [11]. Experts reported successful conjugation of GQDs with antibody and DNA molecules through amide formation that is convenient in the fabrication of nanodevices for highly sensitive diagnostic methods [12].

The optical properties of GQDs are rationalized by modification in their electronic structure. Most of the electronic applications of pristine graphene are hindered by its zero-band gap. Graphene band gap can be tuned by heteroatom doping, modification in size and surface chemistry. GQDs band gap can be engineered by suitable dopants as well as surface functional groups. Feng et al. reported the tuning of electrical properties of GQDs through selective boronization [79]. Chen et al. established a relationship between the size of GQDs and band gap by using density-functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT calculations [80]. Band gap increases when GQDs size is reduced. Modification of GQDs band gap not only studied theoretical but also experimentally. Fig. 9 a shows the GQDs band gap variation with their size [81]. It was confirmed that the GQDs energy band gap depends on size, functionalities and defects in GQDs. In another work, Sarkar and group reported the morphology-dependent band gap of GQDs as shown in Fig. 9b [82]. They also explored the influence of organic functional groups on the GQDs band gap. The analysis showed the shifting of HOMO-LUMO energies without significant change in the HOMO-LUMO gap. A detailed study on the electronic properties of GQDs has been reported by many authors [83–86].

GQDs offer to tune of the band gap by modulating particle size and by modification with heteroatom dopants, chemical groups with defects and edge configuration. These all properties make GQDs a promising material for solar cell and photocatalysis applications. Their wide band gap and flexibility to engineer its band gap over an extensive range and carrier selectivity are the key characteristics that contribute to enhancing the photoconversion efficiency of a variety of solar cells. For photocatalysis, by using heteroatoms like N, P and S it is possible to tune the properties of the GQDs toward the solar absorption property and thus thereby the performance can be optimized. GQDs either by themselves or in combination with other inorganic materials have been used for various photocatalysis reactions like H2 evolution [87–89], degradation of organic pollutants [90–96], CO2 reduction [97,98] and so on. Not only the application, but the introduction of GQDs in solar active materials also open a great opportunity to uncover interesting photophysics. This is why GQDs for solar cells and catalysis applications are one of the burning topics of current and future research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of GQDs in bioimaging and biosensing applications
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of graphene has been already explored by researchers in various fields. To promote the applications of graphene in nanodevices, a novel method is emerged by transforming the 2D graphene sheets into 0D GQDs and tune the band gap of graphene effectively. Due to the quantum confinement and edge effects, GQDs have a promising future in various applications with intrinsic properties of graphene-like high flexibility, thermal conductivity, hardness, high elasticity, etc. [99]. GQDs are carbon-based anisotropic nanomaterials comprising a fabric-like structure that corresponds to graphene. The morphological characteristics of GQDs resemble both carbon dots (CDs) as well graphene [100]. There are two methods for synthesizing the GQDs i.e., top-down and bottom-up approaches. When GQDs are synthesized and fabricated from particularly carbon source materials by various methods like electron beam lithography, chemical oxidation, solvothermal, microwave-assisted hydrothermal, micro-fluidization, and electrochemical techniques. These all above-mentioned methods have come under the top-down approach of synthesis [50,101,102]. On the other hand, the bottom-up synthesizing approach includes stepwise solution chemistry, oxidation and pyrolysis, and hydrothermal heating. Subsequently, larger entities of GQDs are formed by condensing the smaller units of conjugated carbon structures [101]. Furthermore, the synthesized GQDs have magnificent properties like high surface area, larger diameter, using p-p conjugated matrix for better surface grafting or surface groups and other exceptional physical properties. Consequently, GQDs are at the focus of substantial research trials because they are eco-friendly and have low toxicity as compared to QDs of heavy metals which have high toxicity with critical environmental and health concerns [103]. Moreover, the small size of GQDs and high oxygen content contributes towards low cytotoxicity in vitro experiments. Similarly, in vivo biodistribution of GQD in main organs like kidneys showed fast clearance without any accumulation in mice [104]. Experimental and simulations studies resembled well for size-dependent toxicity of GQDs on lipid membranes. Small size GQDs had penetrated the membranes confirming low toxicity whereas larger particles ruptured lipid bilayer efficiently [105]. Owing to their exceptional and tunable optical, electrical and structural characteristics, GQDs are a novel category of molecules that have had an enormous research interest in recent times [106].

With these excellent properties, GQDs are a potential candidate for sensor materials, bioimaging applications, drug delivery systems. Moreover, they are extensively used as fluorescent probes for several analytes because of their substantial properties such as photobleaching resistance, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, stable PL and high aqueous solubility [107]. Furthermore, PL is the most interesting attribute of GQDs that can be easily modified by managing their size, surface functionalization, and chemical doping [108]. Their adjustable and robust PL and confirmed eco-friendly and low toxicity nature make GQDs an attractive candidate for bio-imaging applications. Besides, the surface functionalization phenomenon improved the biocompatibility of GQDs and it is a growing area of research for various applications in the biomedical field [109]. Moreover, GQDs with edge functionalization hold carboxyl, carbonyl, epoxy, and hydroxyl groups which can interconnect with various biological molecules such as proteins, antibodies, enzymes, etc. [110]. Experts reported successful conjugation of GQDs with antibody and DNA molecules through amide formation that is convenient in the fabrication of nanodevices for highly sensitive diagnostic methods [111]. In this section, we have thoroughly discussed the bio-imaging and biosensor-based applications of GQDs.

Bioimaging has a pivotal role in both research and clinical applications and allows learning and observation of subcellular to small animal levels with the help of biological processes [112]. Fluorescent dyes and semiconductor QDs have been extensively used in bioimaging. Although, the poor photostability, low quantum yield, susceptibility to photobleaching of organic dyes and toxicity of heavy metal contained in QDs encourage researchers to find a better alternative for overcome these issues held with organic dyes and QDs [113,114]. Recently, GQDs gained much attention due to high photostability, stable PL, non- or low toxicity and good biocompatibility with excellent fluorescence characteristics. The use of GQDs in bio-imaging has permitted a secluded envision of specific cells and tissues of interest and improved contrast in MR images. The advancement in the scope of functionality of GQDs in bioimaging, they have shown the capability of both fluorescence and MR imaging. Also, its fluorescent properties can be improved or altered with doping or functional groups. Subsequently, GQDs proved to be an ideal candidate for targeting or testing numerous tumor cell lines for bioimaging [109,115]. For in vitro imaging HeLa cell lines have been frequently considered. Moreover, other cell-like dermal fibroblast cells, Chinese hamster ovary CHOeK1 cells, A549 cells, HEK293A cells, Madin-Darby canine epithelial (MDCK) cells, human breast cancer T47D cells, MCF-7 cells, and MC3T3 cells have also been studied [116]. Zhu and co-workers utilized GQDs for traditional bio-imaging by incubation of 104 cells per 150 μL solution of MG-63 (human osteosarcoma) cells with a suspension of 2.5 mg/mL of GQDs from a stock solution with Dulbecco's phosphate buffer saline (DPBS) [70]. Experts suggested that for utilizing in imaging, a high number of GQDs are required into the cells for getting high fluorescence intensity. Jiang et al. prepared luminescent GQDs with high fluorescence quantum yield of 24.6% (excited at 340 nm) and did this most simply by increasing the concentration of GQDs [117]. For instance, Kumawat and co-workers reported a one-pot microwave-assisted green-synthesis route for the fabrication of bright red-luminescent mGQDs using ethanolic extracts of Magniferaindica (mango) leaves. These so-synthesized GQDs exhibit excitation independent fluorescence emission in near-infrared region (NIR) between 650 and 750 nm. Moreover, mGQDs have biocompatibility, photostability and utilized in NIR imaging [118]. Furthermore, Chen and group prepared GQDs from cotton cellulose and the reaction comprised water and cellulose, in the absence of any other hazardous materials. They fabricated GQDs with high PL, favorable hydrophilicity, low cytotoxicity and magnificent biocompatibility and applied them in bio-imaging successfully [119]. Another group studied the functionalization of carboxylated GQDs with glycol corroles through ester bonds. They internalized and distributed the hybrid in live animal cells and evaluated the human breast adenbocarcinoma cell line (MCF-7 cells) by using multiphoton bio-imaging [120]. Ping et al. fabricated pentaethylenehexamine and d-penicillamine co-functionalized GQDs (PEHA-GQD-DPA) by one two-step thermal pyrolysis. The as-synthesized PEHA-GQD-DPA is comprised of graphene sheets with average size of 3.16 nm and the rich in functional groups. It produced high fluorescent quantum yield as 90.91%. The PEHA-GQD-DPA has efficiently used in SW480 cell imaging. Fluorescence imaging of analysis was utilized to prove the importance of PEHA-GQD-DPA in bio-imaging intracellular Hg2+ and glutathione in SW480 cells [121]. In another study, Zhang et al. synthesized nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) from marigold granules that have a 7.84% quantum yield with strong blue fluorescence color. Furthermore, the sensitivity of N-GQDs is demonstrated for concentration Fe3+ in live HeLa cells with fluorescence microscopy and observed that they have good biocompatibility and low toxicity [122]. N-GQDs also come out as biocompatible and label-free fluorescence probes for cellular imaging of human ACHN cells is reported in a study by Kashani and co-workers. They revealed that strong and stable green fluorescence can be even emitted at a high concentration range [64]. Most of the studies reported that GQDs show short-wavelength emission from blue to green. For instance, Xi et al. synthesized N-GQDs from a top-down approach by graphite as a precursor and revealed that these N-GQDs have a 52% total product yield with 88% green-emission as well as 12% blue-emission fluorescence. Also, these N-GQDs own low cytotoxicity and exhibit a good exploration in both in vitro and in vivo bio-imaging. They performed the in vivo imaging investigation of N-GQDs (in different phases like water and DCM i.e., NGQD-w, NGQD-d) for zebrafish as a model. In this study, NGQD-d is selected for in vivo bio-imaging because it has a high fluorescent quantum yield than NGQD-w relatively. In absence of N-GQDs, no fluorescent signal is observed. Although green fluorescence can be observed in some parts of the intestine of zebrafish after consuming and skin absorption of NGQD-d at 10 μg mL−1 concentration during the incubation period. The green fluorescence was also observed in other labeled areas like eyes and stomach on increasing the concentration from 10 to 100 μg mL−1. Unitedly, this study revealed that NGQD-d can be served as fluorescent nanoagent for in vivo bio-imaging applications efficiently in zebrafish and other animals as well [123]. Furthermore Nurunnabi et al. prepared photoluminescent (PL) GQDs via simple exfoliation and oxidation method. For enhancing the stability in water and low toxicity in vivo, they coated these GQDs with polydopamine (pDA). They showed that GQDs coated with pDA exhibited magnificent stability PL intensity. They also revealed that noncoated GQDs in PBS solution reduced swiftly with time, and on incubation of 14 days with PBS solution resulted in 45% reduction in PL intensity. On the other hand, coated GQDs with pDA maintained more stable PL intensities even after 14 days. Moreover, they have analyzed the in vitro and in vivo bio-imaging of pDA-coated GQDs in nude mice. In Fig. 10 , the fluorescence images of GQDs and pDA-coated GQDs after i. v. injection are illustrated and they validated that both GQDs and pDA-coated GQDs were dispersed into each organ tissue i.e., heart, lung, spleen, liver and kidney via organized movement. The results showed that relative fluorescence intensity is low for non-coated GQDs as compared to pDA-coated GQDs (D-GQDs, D-GQD3, D-GQD6, D-GQD12) which have stronger fluorescence intensities in all organ tissues [124].

Furthermore, Singh et al. prepared GQDs from neem roots extract with the help of a simple hydrothermal technique. The as-prepared GQDs showed high biocompatibility, photostability as well as continued full-color emission. In vitro study revealed that the GQDs show a particular attachment towards the lysosomes while in vivo imaging of zebrafish approved that preferred GQDs confined in yolk sac area. These studies indicate that these GQDs can be utilized as fluorescence imaging agents for the digestive system as well [125]. In another study, Wang and co-workers reported the green synthesis of GQDs by the doping of lattice phosphorus (P) with tunable fluorescence and termed as P-GQDs. These P-GQDs have a product yield (71 wt%) and controlled emission wavelength from 457 to 632 nm with a high quantum yield (0.54–0.73). Subsequently, P-GQDs have excellent potential in PL bio-imaging. They demonstrated the various studies in Fig. 11 for different doping concentrations and evaluated these P-GQDs and P-GQDs 1–6 for in vitro imaging of rADSCs cells to investigate cytotoxicity and metabolic activities and found low cytotoxicity. Moreover, PL signal with different colors indicates that all P-GQDs are internalized by cells and can be a type of potential bio-imaging material [126].

GQDs-based biosensors are emerging due to their excellent characteristics like high sensitivity, specificity biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity as efficient bio tools. In addition, they are effectively utilized in various domains like monitoring and assessment of environmental issues, healthcare management, diagnostics, etc. As per experts’ suggestions, the alteration of the electronic structure of the edge groups can change the optical properties of GQDs. Hence, GQDs have a potential role in biosensing applications. The GQDs based biosensor makes use of affinity between analyte biomolecules and functional groups within GQDs. The binding of functional groups associated with GQDs with analyte biomolecules can provide different electronic states whose alteration makes changes in PL intensity can be helpful in analyte detection [115]. The ions necessary for biological processes or accountable for severe toxicity have to be effortlessly conveyed at the cellular level. Therefore, ion in vitro biosensors with high sensitivity and specificity are quite important. Moreover, GQDs-based biosensors have great applicability in disease diagnosis, prognosis, and therapy. Based on transducer type, these biosensors are classified into different categories like optical, electrochemical, photoelectrochemical, etc. [127]. In a study, Li et al. prepared pentaethylenehexamine and histidine-functionalized GQDs (PEHA-GQD-His) and these PEHA-GQD-His served as fluorescence probes for microRNA (miRNA) fluorescence nanoplatforms for biosensing with assembled molecular beacon double-cycle amplification method. The targeted miRNA binds to the molecular beacon specifically to evoke the target cycle and the molecular beacon cycle which results in generation of nanoassembled DNA on the PEHA-GQD-His film. The developed G-quadruplexes accurately fold and fuse hemin to develop stable G-quadruplexes/hemin composites. The study also shows that the framework of target and beacon double cycle generates nanoassembly of DNA on the PEHA-GQD-His film with high specificity. This biosensor could give a fluorescence result with high sensitivity toward miRNA in human serum with a limit of detection (LOD) of 4.3 × 10−19 M and a linearity detection range of 1 × 10−18 to 1 × 10−12 M [128]. Furthermore, a novel fluorescent biosensor platform based on GQDs and pyrene-functionalized molecular beacon probes (py-MBs) was developed by Zhang and group for the quantitative detection of miRNAs. The introduction of pyrene and fluorescent dye dual labeled MBs to strongly absorb on the GQDs film via π−π interactions in the designed platform to trigger the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) specifically. Subsequently, the changes in fluorescence intensity lead to unique signals that are used for targeting the detection of miRNAs. This biosensor platform displayed wide range of detection i.e., 0.1 nM to 200 nM with lower detection limit of 100pM. Besides, this platform has high specificity, good stability, high flexibility and the ability of multiple detection of different miRNAs [129]. Recently, Kalkal and group developed a biofunctionalized GQDs based fluorescent turn-on biosensor for the hypersensitive detection of small cell lung cancer biomarkers with high accuracy. They have utilized a one-pot and bottom-up hydrothermal strategy for the preparation of in-situ amine-functionalized and nitrogen-doped GQDs (amine-N-GQDs). In this study, they immobilized monoclonal neuron-specific enolase antibodies (anti-NSE) covalently onto an amine-N-GQDs surface and results in biofunctionalized GQDs (anti-NSE/amin-N-GQDs). The mechanism of this biosensor is displayed in Fig. 12 a. This platform provided a fast response time within 16 min, a wider linear detection range of 0.1 pg/mL to 1000 ng/mL and a significant LOD of 0.09 pg/mL with outstanding performance in real specimens having an average recovery value of 94.69% [130].

In another study, Cui et al. derived a “turn-on” magnetic fluorescent biosensor based on GQDs, Fe3O4, and MoS2 nanosheets. This platform is used for the fast, effortless, and sensitive detection and separation of tumor cells. Here MoS2 nanosheets are used as fluorescent quenchers and aptamer@Fe3O4@GQD complex was used to fabricate “turn-on” biosensing magnetic fluorescent nanocomposites (MFNs). This platform has substantial efficacy in the detection of tumor cells in whole blood with high sensitivity [131]. In addition, the optical characteristics of GQDs give chances to the utilization of these nanomaterials in the development of photoelectrochemical biosensors where the electron transfer takes place only under the photo-irradiation process. In this regard, Wang and group fabricated a simple and robust aptamer-based photoelectrochemical biosensor for the detection of zeatin with the help of GQDs/graphite-like C3N4 (g- C3N4) nanocomposite system. The role of GQDs was to enhance the photoelectric conversion potential of g- C3N4 because they are photoactivity enhancement reagents. In collaboration with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and DNA biotin assembled aptamer, this biosensor has a broad linear range of 0.1 nM–100 nM with LOD of 0.031 nM. Moreover, this developed strategy also exhibited good selectivity, even discriminating the analogs of zeatin and selectivity can be more enhanced by the introduction of 6-benzylaminopurine, gibberellin, indole-3-acetic acid, indole-3-propionic acid, and indole-3- butyric acid [132].

Based on the strong interaction between single-stranded DNA and graphene material a simple and smart platform is developed by Xiang and group. They developed an electrochemical sensor for the detection of hepatitis B virus (HPV) DNA by using a modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with GQDs which is assembled with specific sequence DNA molecules as a probe. This electrochemical sensor manifested high sensitivity with LOD of 1 nM and linearity range from 10 nM to 500 nM [133]. Furthermore, Mahmoudi-Moghaddam et al. developed an electrochemical approach for the detection of topotecan by evaluation of the interaction between topotecan-DNA on carbon paste electrode (CPE) assisted GQDs. This biosensor was fabricated by functionalization of CPE with GQDs and ionic liquid, double-stranded DNA (ds-DNA). This as-fabricated sensor conveys LOD of 0.1 μM for topotecan via differential pulse voltammetry in a broad range of concentrations from 0.35 to 100 μM. This DNA sensing platform can be applicable for topotecan sensing in human blood and urine as well in clinical setup [134]. As a growing threat of cardiovascular disease (CVD), the fabrication and designing of biosensors for the detection of CVD biomarkers could be the milestone platform among the scientific community. In this regard, Lakshmanakumar and co-workers developed an electrochemical approach for the detection of cardiac Troponin I (cTnI) with the usage of acetic acid functionalized GQDs (fGQDs). They investigated interaction between cTnI and fGQDs for the early detection of myocardial infarction with the help of cyclic voltammetry (CV) and amperometry. In this sensing mechanism, the carboiimide conjugation between the N–H group of cTnI and functionalized COOH group of GQDs allowed the investigation of cTnI biomarker. The Au electrode modified with fGQDs displayed significant electrocatalytic oxidation of cTnI with LOD of 0.02 ng/mL and high sensitivity of linear range from 0.17 to 3 ng/mL [135]. In another study, a novel label-free electrochemical immunosensor was developed by Mollarasouli and team for the specific and sensitive investigation of clinically pertinent biomarker receptor tyrosine kinase (AXL) in human serum. This immunosensing device was fabricated by immobilization of specific anti-AXL antibody onto screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCEs) which are modified with amine functionalized GQDs and represented in Fig. 12b. The as-developed immunosensor has enhanced analytical performance in comparison with other electrochemical immunosensor developed as yet. Moreover, this immunosensor has an extensive range of LOD i.e. 0.5 pg/mL which is more than 1000 times the accepted cut-off value for heart failure (HF) diagnosis in serum (71 ng/mL). Therefore, this developed immunosensor was effectively applied to the investigation of endogenous content of AXL in serum of HF patients without any effect on a network [136].

An excellent diagnostic tool based upon electrochemical strategy fabricated with laccase modified GCE and GQDs for the efficient detection of neurotransmitter epinephrine (EP). This electrochemical platform has a wide linear range of 1–120 × 10−6 M with LOD of 83 nM [136]. Currently, the threat of cancer is increasing exponentially, hence, there is an urgent need for the prognosis of cancer. Consequently, a sensitive label-free immunoassay for the detection of CA15-3 antibodies has been reported. The reported immunosensor was fabricated by the electrochemical assembling of AuNPs onto thiolated GQDs (CysA/GQDs) which offer a maximum surface area for the effective immobilization of CA 15–3 antigens. Moreover, CysA/Au NSs/GQDs hybrid periphery determines the biosafety and stability of immobilized CA 15–3 antigens. This immunosensor detects antibodies as low as 0.11 U/mL with linearity of 0.16–125 U/mL [137]. For instance, Serafin and group developed an integrated electrochemical immunosensor for the first time to detect the IL-13 Rα2 biomarkers. This immunosensor was fabricated by the hybrid of multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) and GQDs and exhibited a linear range from 2.7 to 100 ng/mL with a lower detection value of 0.8 ng/mL for IL-13 Rα2. Also, it depicted high sensitivity and applied efficiently for the direct detection of target receptors in low levels of rare cellular lysates and abstracts of paraffin surrounded tissues of colorectal cancer patients [138]. In another study, GQDs and gold-embedded polyaniline nanowires are used for sensor material in the electrical pulse-induced electrochemical detection of hepatitis E virus (HEV). This platform showed linearity of 102-107 RNA copies mL−1 and detect lower values up to 96.7 RNA copies mL−1 [139]. Furthermore, Ganganboina and Doong developed N, S-GQDs@Au-polyaniline (N, S-GQDs@Au-PANI) nanowires based label-free electrochemical immunosensor for the quantitative detection of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA). This immunosensor shows a wide range of linearity from 0.5 to 1000 ng/mL with LOD of 0.01 ng/mL and exceptional recovery of (96.0 ± 2.6) and (103 ± 3.8) % [140]. A reliable and robust electrochemical biosensor with high sensitivity has been reported for the detection of Mycobacterium tuberculosis antigen (culture filtrate protein; CFP-10). The GCE is altered by the nanotriplex of GQD-coated Fe3O4@Ag core-shell nanostructure (Fe3O4@Ag/GQD) and the platform showed a wide spectrum of linear values from 0.005 to 500 μg/mL with LOD of 0.33 ng/mL [141]. The recently reported GQDs and their nanocomposites-based fluorescence and electrochemical biosensors are listed in Table 2 .

GQDs, the nano-sized layered structured motif of graphene, are growing as a potential candidate for the design and development of efficient drug delivery systems including theragnostic agents. Owing to its low cytotoxicity, richness in peripheral –COOH groups, chemical and thermal stability PL nature, GQDs have been explored in various biomedical and pharmaceutical applications including photothermal therapy, biosensors, drug delivery, molecular imaging, etc. Despite these excellent features, hydrophobicity induced due to its carbon structure is one of the principal challenges to address. Chemical PEGylation, oxidation, reducing the QDs size, etc. are some of the investigated strategies to overcome hydrophobic surface interactions and also to improve the biocompatibility of the GQDs.

In context to their applicability in drug delivery, Wang et al. [18] have first reported the remarkable capability of GQDs not only in delivering the anticancer drug doxorubicin (DOX) but also its contribution in boosting the anticancer activity of the drug against breast cancer MCF-C cells (Fig. 13 A). The GQDs-DOX conjugate is reported to adopt different nuclear and cellular internalization pathways compared to pristine drugs and thus enhancing the drug delivery efficiency (Fig. 13B). Interestingly, these GQDs-DOX conjugates showed a significant increment in the nuclear uptake and cytotoxicity of DOX to the drug-resistant cancer cells demonstrating that the combination of the anticancer drugs with the GQDs may be capable of enhancing the chemotherapeutic efficiency of the anti-cancer drugs that are suboptimal owing to drug resistance. Ko et al. [143] have developed GQDs based nanocarrier labeled with Herceptin (HER) and β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) for breast cancer diagnosis and treatment. In these nanovesicles, HER acted as an active targeting agent to HER2-overexpressed breast cancer, β-CD provided hydrophobic drug loading site for doxorubicin (DOX) via host-guest chemistry and GQDs performed as diagnostic agents due to its pH-sensitive blue emission in the acidic environment of cancer cells. These smartly designed GQDs based multifunctional drug delivery carriers lead to a synergistically improved anticancer approach with both diagnosis and treatment. Dong et al. [144] have designed DOX loaded amino acid (AA) conjugated GQDs with stable fluorescence properties and utilized them for dye-free imaging as well as for tracking and monitoring drug delivery (Fig. 13C). The inherent fluorescence properties of the GQDs permit efficient real-time monitoring of cellular uptake of DOX-GQDs-AA nanocarriers and subsequent drug release. Also, the nano-assemblies showed pH-dependent drug release characteristics.

To implement multiple operations simultaneously including selective cellular imaging, targeted drug delivery and real-time monitoring of drug cellular uptake, Wang et al. [20] have developed and demonstrated smart nanoassemblies constituted of folic acid (FA) functionalized GQDs loaded with DOX. These nanoassemblies are found to specifically internalize by the HeLa cells via a receptor (FA) mediated endocytosis, with controlled prolonged release of DOX, while exhibiting minimal toxicity to the non-targeted cells. Owing to the excellent fluorescence properties of the GQDs, the movement of FA-GQDs-DOX nanocarriers can be easily monitored in the cells without employing any external dyes. Recently, Ju et al. have reported a charge reversal N-GQDs-DOX-APTES nuclear targeting multifunctional drug delivery nanocarriers for cancer therapeutics [145]. While designing the nanocarriers, the charge reversal agent APTES is grafted onto the N-GQDs-DOX and this charge reversal property of APTES enhances the uptake of drug DOX specifically by the cell nuclei for the improved safe treatment. The result obtained from the in-vitro cell studies reveals that the N-GQDs-DOX-APTES improve both the nuclear targeting drug delivery and the photodynamic therapy (PDT). For the treatment of prostate cancer, multifunctional GQDs based drug delivery nanocarriers functionalized with targeting molecules arginine-glycine-aspartic acid (RGD) and DOX were reported for the simultaneous tracking and targeting treatment of tumor cells [146]. The RGD peptides specifically bind to the “ανβ3” antigens overexpressed in cancer cells and promotes the internalization, intracellular distribution and release (Fig. 13D). The intrinsic fluorescent properties of GQDs allow the surveillance of the whole set of processes involved in drug release. Eventually, the acidification of the internalizing vesicles triggers the DOX release from GQDs. In another interesting study, Huang et al. have designed paramagnetic GQDs, named folate-GdGQDs, by covalently conjugating diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid gadolinium and folic acid onto the surface of GQDs (Fig. 13E) [147]. An in vitro HeLa and HepG2 cell viability assay and in vivo evaluation of toxicity to the embryonic development of zebrafish showed that this folate–GdGQDs exhibited excellent biocompatibility within the given range of concentrations. More importantly, strong therapeutic activity was achieved by loading DOX onto folate–GdGQDs surfaces via π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions, leading to the formation of folate–GdGQD/Dox multifunctional nanocarriers. The carriers release ∼80% of the drug under mild acidic conditions (pH 5.0), whereas only 20% of DOX was released at pH 7.0 after 48 h.

Iannazzo et al. [150] have reported the synthesis of highly water dispersible biocompatible GQDs based drug delivery nanovector with targeted ligand riboflavin (RF) for targeted delivery of benzofuran G (BFG) drug. The binding of the drug to GQDs was mediated using a cleavable PEG linker and the RF was linked to the GQDs via π-π∗ interactions employing pyrene linker. The cytotoxicity of the GQDs-PEG-BFG@Pyr-RF was evaluated against three different cancer cell lines and the findings confirm excellent cytotoxicity of the GQD-PEG-BFG@Pyr-RF DDS against all the investigated tumor cell lines with the potency significantly higher in alveolar cancer cells compared to BFG alone. Hodadadei et al. [151] have reported the synthesis of nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs, 10 nm) by hydrothermal method using citric acid and urea as carbon and nitrogen source, respectively. For the first time, authors have reported the development of cancer-targeting drug delivery vesicles using N-GQDs loaded with methotrexate (MTX) drugs. The cytotoxicity evaluation using the MTT assay against human breast cancer cells confirmed high cytotoxicity of the MTX enriched N-GQDs for tumor cells whereas drug-free nanocarriers are found to be biocompatible. Li et al. [148] have developed carboxylated GQDs (c-GQDs) based drug delivery vesicles modified with NH2-poly(ethylene glycol)-NH2 and folic acid targeting human cervical cancer cells. Interestingly, this PEGylated c-GQDs based DDS showed excellent drug entrapment efficiency and drug loading capacity of 97.5% and 40.1%, respectively. The in-vivo studies confirmed the outstanding anti-tumor properties of the drug delivery nanosystem with negligible systemic toxicity.

In another interesting study by Some et al. [149], the anticancer activity of different curcumin-loaded graphene derivatives has been investigated. Compared to the GO-Curcumin (Cur) composite, double oxidized GO (DGO)-Cur composite showed increased anticancer activity which is due to enhanced drug loading owing to the availability of more oxygen-rich functional groups in DGO structure. Furthermore, GQD-Cur composite showed maximum potential to inhibit the tumor cells and serves as a probe for tumor imaging due to its high surface area. Remarkably, the authors have declared ultrahigh drug-loading capacity of curcumin ∼40,800 mg/g for GQDs, which is the highest ever reported for any nanomaterial-based nanocarrier. As advanced theranostic nanocarrier, Su et al. [152] have developed size changeable GQD nanoaircrafts (SCNA) that serve as a hierarchical tumor-targeting agent with a high cargo payload to penetrate and deliver a drug to deep tumors (Fig. 14 A). The nanoaircrafts are composed of GQDs (<5 nm) coated with a pH-responsive polymer that undergoes aggregation transition at weak acidic conditions in the tumor environment, however, the carriers are stable at physiological pH with stealth function. The conversion in size of the nanoaircrafts is further stimulated by near-infrared irradiation which unassembled the 150 nm SCNA into 5 nm of DOX/GQD nanostructures, enabling their infiltration into deep tumor cells for repeated effective cell killing. Such smart nanocarriers with the integration of combinational therapeutics is found to successfully suppresses xenograft tumors within 18 days without any digital damage. This sophisticated approach shows the hierarchically targeted and penetrated drug delivery and energy to a deep tumor and shows budding potential for use in overall cancer therapy. In another report on combinational cancer therapy, light-responsive GQDs based nanoconstructs have been designed to deliver cancer treatment via Biological Redox-Enhanced photodynamic therapy (PDT) Combined with photothermal Heating [153]. The GQDs were employed as multifunctional nanocarriers to load lutetium (III) texaphyrin (Lu-TP) for PDT and Gadolinium (III) texaphyrin (Gd-TP) which is a tumor-selective redox drug. The GQDs were further conjugated to cyclic peptide, c(RGDyK) (cyclo(Arg-Gly-Asp-D-Phe-Lys)) to enhance the selectivity of the overall Lu-TP & Gd-TP/GQD-RGD drug delivery system towards the cancer cells. The biological redox enhancement by Gd-TP intensely enhances the PDT competence of Lu-TP, producing fast cancer cell death at very small doses of PDT. Further compounding this method with the light-triggered mild photothermal therapy (PTT) of GQDs advances the biological redox-enhanced PDT efficacy remarkably. Because of the synergistic therapy of biological redox therapy (BRT), PDT, and PTT (Fig. 14B), the nanoconstruct efficiently suppresses tumor growth under low-power light at 765 nm. This system enables the assessment of the cancer therapy by dual-modality enhanced imaging. Table 3 sumamries the drug delivery behavior of GQDs-based drugs (see Fig. 15).

The emergence of antibiotic-resistant bacteria is one of the consistently growing threats to the well-being of humanity [156]. To exemplify, β-lactam antibiotics are the most frequently prescribed antibiotics in the hospital settings; however, the bacteria form β-lactamases enzymes to develop resistance [157]. In such a scenario, efficient antimicrobials alternatives are highly essential. In the exploration of new antimicrobials, graphene and graphene derivatives are investigated enormously for their action towards combating bacterial growth and now they are known for their antimicrobial activity for nearly a decade [154,155,158]. Generation of oxygen stress is the key mechanism of action behind the killing of the bacterial cells for Graphene and its derivatives. Findings from the earlier studies revealed that among all the graphene derivatives, GO owing to generating more oxidative stress via substantial glutathione oxidation has the best antimicrobial properties, which got further improved by decreasing the lateral size of the GO sheets [159,160]. The functionalized GQDs are unveiled as an effective antimicrobial material. The large π-conjugated system of GQDs can easily bind with the bacterial cell wall via e– transfer, creating membrane stress by generating the ROS, which leads to the leakage and disruption of the cell barriers resulting in cell death [161]. Nevertheless, the unmodified GQDs are found to have very low antimicrobial activity. For example, in a report by Biswas et al. the authors have synthesized GQDs from the multi-walled carbon nanotubes and their antimicrobial action was evaluated against Staphylococcus aureus, Bacillus subtilis, Escherichia coli, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa bacteria [162]. As per the findings, high minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) values were reported for all the investigated bacterial strains i.e., 256 μg mL−1 for B. subtilis and E. coli and, around 512 μg mL−1 for S. aureus and P. aeruginosa.

The GQDs based composite system with polymer modifications and/or with specific metal integration are found to enhance the antimicrobial potential of the QDs since the polymers help in enhancing the proximity of the QDs to the bacterial cell membrane and metal nanoparticles bind with the –SH groups of the proteins and enzymes of the cell barrier. In this context, Habiba et al. have reported complete bacterial inhibition for S. aureus and P. aeruginosa on incubating them with 25 μg mL−1 and 50 μg mL−1 of PEGylated GQDs decorated Ag nanoparticles (Ag-NPs) for 8 h [163]. Authors have also studied the antimicrobial synergism between AgNPs and GQDs and found a low fractional inhibitory concentration (FIC) index value (<0.5), indicating that there is a synergistic effect between AgNPs and GQDs which leads to better antimicrobial effect. Furthermore, 100% cell viability was reported for the mammalian cell on treating them with Ag-GQDs, confirming their excellent biocompatibility. Recently, Liu et al. have emphasized the worth of functional nanocomposites of GQDs and semiconducting ZnO nanoparticles in prohibiting the growth of E. coli cells upon UV treatment and the antimicrobial efficacy of the composite is reported to be better relative to the ZnO and GQDs separately [164]. This was attributed to the boosted production of ROS upon UV photoirradiation, with slight contributions from membrane impairment, as manifested in fluorescence microscopic electron and paramagnetic resonance measurements. Zeng et al. have reported the fabrication of nano-carbon functionalized membrane with excellent bactericidal and antibiofouling properties by covalent binding of the GOQDs onto –NH2 modified PVDF membrane for surface water treatment and biomolecules separation (Fig. 13) [165]. The GOQDs coating on the PVDF membrane significantly inhibits the S. aureus and E. coli growth, prevents the biofilm formation and provided superior antifouling properties to the membrane than previously reported for 2D graphene sheets and 1D CNT impregnated membranes. This is attributed to the uniform homogenous and intense dispersion of the GOQDs, permitting the exposure of a large fraction of the active edges to the bacterial cells leading to the generation of high oxidation stress. Furthermore, the GOQDs modified membranes are described to have long-term stability and durability.

In progress to this, GQDs and their doped counterparts have shown enormous potential in photodynamic antimicrobial therapy. In this context, a very interesting study was reported by Kuo et al. where N-GQDs were used as photosensitizers and a high bactericidal effect has been generated against gram-negative E. coli bacteria using 670-nm laser exposure (0.1 W cm−1) for only 3 min owing to the production of a higher amount of reactive oxygen species [166]. The incubation of the bacterial cells with N-GQDs having 5% nitrogen content is found to be responsible for 100% bacterial killing whereas 51% bacterial inhibition is reported for undoped GQDs. In comparison to the lower N content (2.9%), the GQDs with higher nitrogen content are found to have superior PDT efficiency and improved bactericidal properties. Moreover, the intrinsically emitted luminescence from nitrogen-doped GQDs and high photo stability enabled it to act as a potential contrast probe for tracking and localizing the bacteria in biomedical imaging. In another study by Kholikov et al. the antimicrobial activity of sulfur doped GQDs in combination with methylene blue (MB-GQDs) was unveiled against E. coli and Micrococcus luteus bacteria due to the improvement in the production of the single oxygen species and photoresponsivity [161]. The authors have found 100% killing of the bacterial cells within 10 min of irradiation. Biswas et al. have documented enhanced toxicity of the surface disordered rutile TiO2/GQD hybrid materials against E. coli, S. aureus and P. aeruginosa bacterial cells when equated with the pristine TiO2 and GQD [167]. This TiO2/GQD hybrid material is described to have low MIC values (60 μgmL−1) and having capability to disrupt biofilms upon UV light exposure. Table 4 summaries the antimicrobial properties of GQDs-based materials.
