Phototherapy is a non-invasive medical treatment that involves fluorescent light for the treatment of various diseases. It is divided into two parts photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) respectively (see Fig. 16
). While performing diagnosis, the therapeutic agent is delivered to the disease site (tumors), and after that, the agent is photoexcited by irradiating the light at a defined wavelength. In PTT, the photothermal agent (PT) absorbs near-infrared (NIR) light to produce heat, leading to the ablation of cells [168]. On the other hand, PDT involves photosensitizers (PS) and is activated by light to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), which in turn damages the cells [169]. The primary concern associated with traditional radio- and chemotherapies is the raise of systemic side effects when applied against tumors. Nanomaterials developed for PTT and PDT have shown great potential due to their excellent properties, significantly more efficient therapeutic behavior as well as minimal side effects [170,171]. The evolution of next-generation therapeutic agents promises phototherapy as an ideal treatment against tumors. The phototherapy treatment not only ablates the cancer cells but also helps in improving the efficiency of various therapeutic modalities like radiotherapy, gene therapy and chemotherapy, respectively. In particular, the use of PTT improves the therapeutic effect of chemo and radiotherapies. PTT creates hypothermia that enhances intra tumoural blood flow which improves the oxygen flow in tumors. Further, the drug cellular uptake and release also will be enhanced by increasing the cell membrane permeability at the tumor sites [172]. Similarly, the ROS generated by PDT also promotes drug delivery at the tumor site and inhibits the drug efflux from tumors to improve the efficiency of anticancer drugs [173] (see Fig. 17).

The use of various photothermal (PT) and photosensitizers (PS) agents can efficiently improve phototherapies. These agents may include carbon derivatives, gold nanostructures, metal nanoparticles, metal sulfide nanoparticles, semiconducting polymer quantum dots and 2D oxide nanoparticles respectively. These nanomaterials are used as potential phototherapeutic agents, especially for the treatment of cancer [174]. In comparison, carbon materials with appropriate sizes and large surface areas are suitable as carriers for the delivery of therapeutic agents. The successful use of carbon nanomaterials (e.g. carbon nanotubes carbon nano horns, graphene) in photo therapies can be attributed to their inherent strong absorption nature in the wavelength range of 750–1700 nm. Besides, the carbon nanomaterials selectively accumulate at tumor sites due to their more excellent permeability and retention properties. Having appropriate size allows the nanomaterials to acquire these properties and facilitate them to become ideal candidates for numerous therapeutic applications. Further, amongst all other carbon nanomaterials, the GQDs have been in prime focus over the past decade as they inherit several excellent properties as phototherapeutic agents. The inherent properties of GQDs include easily tuneable fluorescence properties, good photo-and chemical stability, excellent solubility and high biocompatibility [175]. Though GQDs have unique and useful properties; research is still in its infancy and has to be understood well in all the exploited and unexploited applications.

In general, GQDs are made up of one or more graphene layers. Each layer thickness is equivalent to the single atomic thickness, and the atoms are packed into a planar honeycomb crystal structure. This kind of arrangement yield greater thermal and chemical stability to the nanostructures. As mentioned before, GQDs have attracted a lot of attention due to their chemical and photo stability, superior water solubility and ease of fabrication [176]. The biocompatible and non-toxic behavior of GQDs can be attributed to their relatively small sizes (<20 nm) which facilitate them to be cleared and excreted easily via kidneys. The low cytotoxic [177] biocompatible [178] and the ability to generate ROS upon photoexcitation enable the use of GQDs for phototherapy. Moreover, GQDs can transfer electrons to photothermal agents and conjugated drugs which can synergistically enhance their anticancer activity [179]. Having such exceptional properties enabled GQDs to find applications in bioimaging, drug delivery, electronics, optics, sensors, photodynamic and photothermal therapies.

In addition to photoluminescent properties, GQDs can transfer electrons either as donors or acceptors [180]. When irradiated, GQDs with passively surface coated polyethylene glycol (PEG) were able to either quench or produce ROS [181]. This suggested that GQDs in association with a photosensitizer are potential candidates for PDT by producing ROS (singlet oxygen (1O2)) to kill tumor cells [182]. PDT has been recognized as a non-invasive (minimally invasive) and low-toxic targeted therapeutic strategy for the destruction of cancerous tissues [182]. Since PDT is a non-invasive and non –specific process, the nearby cells of the tumor also undergo the same oxidative damage as that of tumor cells irrespective of their toxic status [71,183]. Therefore, cell targeting is also an essential issue while developing the potential photodynamic agent for phototherapy. The cytotoxicity of GQDs is mediated by inducing oxidative stress via photodynamic path and subsequent activation of both Type I/apoptosis and Type II/autophagy cell deaths respectively [184].

Multi-functional core-shell nanoparticles composed of up conversion nanoparticles as core and graphene oxide QDs as shell provided desirable functions for cell imaging, drug delivery and phototherapy. Hypocrellin A, a commonly used chemotherapeutic drug and photosensitizer is conjugated on QDs by π-π interactions, facilitate the PDT with minimal side effects [185]. When photoexcited (470 nm, 1 W), electrochemically produced GQDs showed antibacterial activity against two pathogenic strains, methicillin-resistant staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli [186]. Jovanovic et al. have investigated the ability of γ-irradiation on photoluminescent properties of GQDs [187]. The primary outcome is that GQDs act as better photo producers when irradiated at the lower dose of γ-irradiation against the higher dose of γ-irradiation, suggesting that low-dose irradiated GQDs are promising candidates for PDT.

Targeted theranostic GQDs attached with aptamer AS1411 were developed by Wang et al., in 2015 [188]. The use of aptamer allows GQDs to specifically target tumor cells with high specificity and kill cells even at minimal dosage. While targeting tumors, the aptamer is complemented by GQDs due to their excellent features such as size, fluorescence, and biocompatibility and NIR responsiveness. In PDT, the solubility of photosensitizers is an issue while targeting the tumor cells. The hydrophobic nature of the molecules like Ce 6 and verteporfin (porphyrin derivatives) reprimand their use as photosensitizers [189]. These solubility issues can be addressed by conjugating the porphyrin derivatives, i.e. photosensitizers to PEG and subsequently, GQDs will be concealed within the conjugated PEG shells. Thus, synthesized PEGylated GQDs can be redox triggered for PDT. Further, the adhesion of photosensitizers improves their hydrophilicity without affecting the photodynamic efficiency and thereby avoiding rapid clearance from the system as well. The use of PEGylated GQDs has vastly enhanced bioimaging, biocompatibility and yielded high therapeutic efficiency during PDT [190]. In a similar study; PEGylated GQDs were conjugated with a porphyrin derivative. Thus formed PEGylated GQDs were found to be stable and have excellent biocompatible nature when tested on MCF-7 and A549 cancer cells. Polydopamine has also been used to stabilize GQDs-porphyrin derivative (Ce6) nano assembly which after that induces tumor cell ablation by enhancing photodynamic effects. The nano assembly facilitates Tlymocyte infiltration by targeting Toll-like receptor 9 on tumor cells [191].

Besides improving the solubility of photosensitizers, the GQDs facilitate the multi-stage sensitization for achieving higher quantum yields. The use of GQDs based PDT nano agents can generate ROS with a very high quantum yield (∼1.3) [192]. This can be achieved by following a new ROS-generating mechanism through multi-stage sensitization. In vivo mouse models have confirmed the fluorescence imaging capability of GQDs. When irradiated with light, GQDs have destroyed the tumors in mice which have not regrown even after 17 days. In another study, a multi-functional two-photon nanoprobe was designed and tested. GQDs and MnO2 nano sheets were coupled to amplify ROS quantum yield in PDT and detect intra tumor glutathione release in cancer cells [193].

The GQDs are also used as potent NIR-responsive photothermal agents due to their unique optical absorbance and tunable fluorescence emission characteristics [176]. GQDs have strong optical absorption at NIR-I (700–950 nm) region and low absorption at the longer NIR-II (1000–1700 nm) wavelength region respectively. This limits the GQDs usage in specific PTT applications. However, studies have been done to enhance the absorption potential of GQDs in higher wavelengths. In the presence of the high magnetic field of 9 T, the GQDs (9TGQDs) have shown outstanding absorption at 1070 nm in the NIR-II region. In addition to elevated absorption, magnetization has yielded a high quantum yield of fluorescence (16.67%) and photothermal conversion (33.45%) [176].

Li, S. et al. reported the loading capacity of theranostic agents IR780 by functionalizing GQDs with folic acid to form GQDs-FA nanostructures [194]. The IR780 provides simultaneous phototherapeutic and fluorescent imaging. The GQDs-FA ability was attributed to the existence of carboxyl groups around the edge of an intact sp2 domain. This arrangement has facilitated the strong π–π stacking interactions with IR780. The presence of carboxyl groups on the surface further enhances the solubility of IR780 in water boy over 2400-fold. Having such a molecular arrangement has improved the efficacy of IR780/GQDs-FA in tumor targeting, photostability and also yielded 87.9% of photothermal conversion efficiency. These properties facilitate sufficient hyperthermia against tumor cells and completely eradicate them when irradiated by an 808 nm laser. Sustainable and eco-friendly GQDs were prepared from withered leaves of fig trees [195]. In situ labeling probes were designed using the developed GQDs and tested on cancer cells. Labeling of cancer cells resulted in a tremendous improvement in the generation of ROS when irradiated with NIR laser having 808 nm wavelengths. Even after irradiating for 30 min, GQDs maintained high stability towards PL. In another study, the bioavailability of nanomaterials was prolonged by adhering PEG to them [196]. Under in vivo conditions, PEG has improved the circulation time of nanomaterials in blood by reducing their interaction with monocytes and macrophages. PEGylation has also been used to enhance the loading capacity of doxorubicin (DOX), a chemotherapeutic drug, in GQDs [183]. The efficacy of drug-loaded GQDs was tested on HeLa and DU145 cancer cells. After cell targeting, the synergistic effect of drug-photosensitizer has caused cell death after irradiating the tumor with 425 nm radiation. When amino group functionalized, GQDs were doped with nitrogen, having generated high quantum yield (33%) at ∼800 nm irradiation [197]. Doping with nitrogen has helped in eradicating Staphylococcus aureus, methicillin-resistant bacteria by generating a higher amount of ROS. In comparison, stronger PDT was observed for nitrogen-doped GQDs as compare to the undoped GQDs. This behavior is parallel to other GQDs that exhibited photo dynamically active and antimicrobial properties to use against bacteria successfully [161].

GQDs are capable of absorbing NIR light waves and emit sufficient heat to ablate cancer cells [198]. The synergistic effect of drug and heat was created by capping GQDs with mesoporous silica nanoparticles [199]. When irradiated under NIR or magnetic field, the GQDs concurrently released heat and DOX too. In another study, DOX embedded in a multi-functional zeolite imidazolate framework of GQDs was studied. When the photoexcited and under an acidic environment, the DOX was released [198]. Light sensitive (to NIR and generate heat), pH-sensitive and temperature-sensitive mesoporous silica nanoparticles coated GQDs were developed to release DOX against cancer cells [200].

Preclinical and clinical studies have indicated that monotherapy for cancer is not as effective as expected due to the facts like drug resistance, metastasis of cancer, individual differences in patients, and so on. As the name suggested, combination therapy integrates the principles of more than one kind of therapeutic approach. It synergistically enhances the potential of each system that results in better cancer treatment. Henceforth, the combination of phototherapy has displayed more significant benefits for cancer treatment. The combined PTT and PDT using carbon nanomaterials by amalgamating with different photosensitizers have received considerable attention in recent years. To achieve this combined phototherapy (PTT/PDT) of cancer, hydrophilic macromolecules such as proteins, polymers, or polysaccharides, have to be attached to carbon nanomaterials. Subsequently, photosensitizers are conjugated to their surface via covalent or non-covalent (π-π interactions) bonds. GQDs-based phototherapy materials are summarized in Table 5
.

In general, GQDs possess intrinsic photothermal and photodynamic properties (see Table 6). Photoluminescent GQDs prepared by Lee et al., in 2014, include both the properties and can be used for both cancer phototherapy and bioimaging respectively [201]. When irradiated at 670 nm wavelength of light, the photothermal effect is generated from electronic vibration via electron clouds. The photodynamic effect has resulted from the generation of ROS after irradiation, which triggers a structure analogous to pheophorbide-A.

Photocatalysis is a process where the rate of a chemical reaction can be significantly improved by a catalyst in presence of light. It is an important branch of science for a sustainable future where the world is looking at renewable energy sources as alternatives to the current conventional fossil fuel-based energy source. However, the major challenge for photocatalysis is first to develop a catalyst that can efficiently absorb the sunlight leading to enhanced chemical reaction and second is to develop an efficient photocatalyst by cost-effective process and on a bulk scale. The key parameters that define the efficiency of the material for catalysis application are the electronic structure, morphology and atomic structure. Given unique properties of GQDs like high stability, high solubility, enhanced surface area, good conductivity along with non-toxicity makes GQDs a promising material for photocatalysis as well as for electrocatalysis applications. Along with this, GQDs offer tuning of the band gap by modulating particle size and by modification with heteroatom dopants, chemical groups with defects and edge configuration. These all properties make GQDs a promising material. For photocatalysis, by using heteroatoms like N, P and S it is possible to tune the properties of the GQDs toward the solar absorption property and thus thereby the performance can be optimized. GQDs either by themselves or in combination with other inorganic materials have been used for various photocatalysis reactions like H2 evolution [87–89], degradation of organic pollutants [90–96], CO2 reduction [97,98], and so on.

Lee and group [203] developed a novel ultrasonic process for the synthesis of GQDs which possess excitation independent down conversion and up conversion photoluminescent property. They further synthesized, GQDs composites with rutile and anatase phase of titanium oxide (TiO2) and used these GQDs derived composites for the degradation of methylene blue dye under visible light. It was demonstrated that rutile TiO2/GQDs composite exhibits approximately 9% higher photocatalytic degradation rate for methylene blue compared to anatase TiO2/GQD composite on illumination with the visible light with a wavelength higher than 420 nm. Wang et al. [204] demonstrated the process for the synthesis of the hydroxyl-GQDs (OH-GQDs) and combined OH-GQDs with the mesoporous carbon nitride (mpg-C3N4) using the electrostatic interaction method. The composite developed was observed to significantly improve the photodegradation of pollutants namely Rhodamine B and tetracycline hydrochloride in presence of visible light. Electron spin resonance spectroscopy results showed that the photocatalysis reaction proceed by generating O2.- species and also by production of holes. Higher efficiency of OH-GQDs/mpg-C3N4 over mpg-C3N4 was attributed to the synergy between OH-GQDs and mpg-C3N4, where GQDs get uniformly disperses on the surface of mpg-C3N4 and thereby enhances the photocatalytic performance by acting as electron transfer channels. Yeh and group [98] have demonstrated engineered GQDs with narrow band gap and thus with improved absorption of visible light for enhanced H2 evolution and CO2 reduction. The group chemically modified the GQDs, with electron-withdrawing groups (such as –COOH) and electron-donating group (such as –NH2), which lead to the development of GQDs with p-type and n-type conductivity respectively. They proposed that in these GQDs with functional groups the basal network of carbon acts as ohmic contact between n-type and p-type GQDs and thereby results in unique Z-scheme structure. Together they form a nature like Z-scheme structure with efficient p-n type intramolecular photochemical reaction. This GQDs based artificial Z-scheme structure was shown to efficiently produce H2 and also reduced CO2 significantly.

Jang et al. [205] developed a hydrothermal synthesis process whereby they got GQDs decorated ZnS nanobelts and further used this material for photodegradation of Rhodamine dye. They further optimized the ratio of GQD/ZnS to get the maximum efficiency and it was reported to be a mass ratio of 8 × 10−4. The optimized GQD/ZnS nanocomposite was demonstrated to possess a photodegradation rate constant of 0.0046 min−1, which was reported to be 14 times higher than commercially available ZnS powder. Here, GQDs act as a collector as well as a transporter of an electron in presence of light thereby increases the photocatalytic activity by efficiently blocking the electron-hole combination. Mahdi and group [206] used a one-step electrochemical method to fabricate GQDs, which were then uniformly decorated on anodized ZnO nanowires in different concentrations. The developed composite where GQD/ZnO composite with 0.4 wt percent of GQDs was reported to exhibit three times higher photocatalytic activity compared to pure ZnO nanowires. It was proposed that the reaction proceeds following a Z-scheme approach where GQD play important role in charge carrier generation, separation and transport when exposed to visible light. In another study by Lei et al. [207], Ti3+self-doped TiO2/GQDs nanosheets were synthesized using the calcination method. The developed material exhibited significantly higher photodegradation for the methylene blue and rhodamine B compared to bare TiO2. It was proposed that while Ti3+ was found to be efficient in the separation of charge carriers generated, GQDs were significantly contributing to the efficient charge transfer process across the TiO2 thereby facilitating the higher photocatalytic response. In similar progress [207], of using GQDs composites, CdS/GQDs nanohybrid have been demonstrated as an efficient strongly coupled photocatalyst that possessed significant photocatalytic activity for the H2 production. Compared to CdS only, the CdS/GQDs nanohybrid composites containing 1.0 wt % of GQDs exhibited 2.7 times higher photocatalytic efficiency. The increased performance was attributed to the enhanced light absorption by GQDs. Given the extraordinary ability of GQDs to significantly improved the visible light response for the photocatalytic activity, several groups have developed and demonstrated GQDs based composites with materials like Bi2MoO6 [208], ZnO [206], TiO2 [92,96] that have been shown to exhibit much higher photocatalytic activity in presence of GQDs as compared to bare oxides.

In the last decade, the GQDs based materials have also been found to be an interesting candidate as a photocatalyst for organic synthesis as well. GQDs, when combined with the formate dehydrogenase, resulted in a higher formation rate of formic acid by CO2 reduction in presence of light [209]. Similarly, Fe (III) tetra (4-sulfonatophenyl)porphyrins in combination with N,S doped GQDs was found to efficient photocatalyst for oxidation of the alcohol to carbonyl compounds [93]. Different groups have also developed GQDs-organic polymer and GQDs-organic/inorganic polymer composites for various photocatalytic applications. Ye and group [210] synthesized GQDs-polyvinyl pyrrolidone-CdS nanocomposite by one-step hydrothermal process. The as-prepared composite was reported to efficiently degrade the methyl orange under visible light. The higher photocatalytic activity was due to the GQDs upconversion property with efficient charge transfer between CdS and GQDs. Polymer-modified GQDs have also been developed and were demonstrated to show an efficient means for the degradation of methylene blue under light irradiation [211]. As a polymer, polyethylenimine and polyethylene glycol were used. These studies further revealed that the choice of the polymer can result in the GQDs/polymer composites with a wide range of absorption in presence of light thereby can be used for various applications like photocatalytic degradation of organic compounds, sewage treatment, and drug release and so on.

To date, GQDs are not significantly exposed to fuel cell technology. However, these are limitedly/rarely used in different types of fuel cells including solar cells, direct methanol fuel cells, and microbial fuel cells [213–218]. However, GQDs are significantly used as photo/electro-catalyst for different energy applications including oxygen reduction reaction and hydrogen evolution reaction [219]. Here, we will only discuss the aforesaid fuel cell.

As an alternative source of energy, direct methanol/ethanol fuel cells are considered as an alternative source of energy. These are comparatively safe and accessible than hydrogen/proton exchange membrane fuel cell as these could be used in the portable application at ambient temperature. Also, no external humidification is required contrasting to the hydrogen fuel cell [213,220,221]. Shukla et al. (2019) have demonstrated the simplistic preparation of graphene nanoribbon-GQD composite and its application in direct methanol fuel cells as an electrolyte membrane. They showed a favorable constructive effect on the said composite membrane by sulfur doping as they facilitate the proton exchange and enhance the proton conductivity. They prepared graphene nanoribbon-GQD composite by grafting of GQD on graphene nanoribbon followed by sulfonation using benzene diazonium sulfonate precursor. For casting the membrane for direct methanol fuel cell they used sulfonated aromatic polymer substrate, sulfonated poly(ether ether ketone) as an alternative of Nafion 117 and graphene nanoribbon-GQD composite in three different ratios (1, 1.5 and 2 wt%). The result showed 1.5 wt% ratio of composite has better physico-electrochemical properties for the cell including SEM, AFM, XRD, durability, FTIR and EIS. They concluded 1.5 wt% combinations can generate higher power density (40% more than Nafion 117) as shown in Fig. 18 along with more stability. They also claimed the prepared GQDs based electrolyte membrane could be commercialized as an alternative to expensive other polymer electrolyte membranes [213].

Xu and his co-workers (2020) have tried to develop an efficient GQDs reinforced proton exchange membrane for the direct methanol fuel cell [218]. It has already been proven that the Nafion membrane is being preferred in direct methanol fuel cell due to its relatively high proton conductivity, mechanical strength, stability and sufficient perfluorosulfonic acid. However, low methanol/proton selectivity and high cost are the main interruptions in the commercialization of direct methanol fuel cells [222,223]. They synthesized multiple branches of polyamide-based proton-conducting membrane doped with GQDs. They confirmed that functional groups (oxygenated) of GQDs bind with the SO3H group of the membrane via hydrogen bonding resulting in fast proton transfer/conductivity in direct methanol fuel cells. The effectiveness of the dispersed GQDs in the electrolyte membrane of the fuel cell was proved by several experiments including proton conductivity, methanol permeability, oxidative and mechanical stability and TGA. The results showed GQDs are responsible for 17% enhancement in proton conductivity than the pristine electrolyte membrane. One more interesting finding was observed. These GQDs are responsible for 80% more mechanical stability of the membrane [218].

Apart from the chemical fuel cell, GQDs alone and a combination of heteroatoms are being used in a bio-electrochemical fuel cell to make them more competent [217]. Habibi et al. showed that composite of FeS2–S, N GQDs based anode was more capable of dense biofilm and electron transfer. It is known that heteroatoms lone-pairs always eases the electron transfer on anode surface and FeS2 is responsible for crucial energy-transporting foundations for biofilm-growth. The FeS2–S, N GQDs anode-based microbial fuel cell showed 76% higher power density than carbon cloth electrode-based fuel cell. FeS2–S, N GQDs based anode favors redox reactions of cytochromes in microbes and displays higher power output and stability than the traditional carbon cloth [217]. This study reveals the use of GQDs and their combination with heteroatoms as a novel electrocatalyst for bioelectricity generation.

The application of GQDs offers a simple and effective route to enormously boost the power conversion efficiencies of a range of solar cells. GQDs display wide band gap, carrier selectivity, and tunable electrical properties which all are important for their application in solar cells. The GQDs can be deployed as carrier selective window layers in solar cells or inclusions of GQDs in traditional window layers can enhance their performance. Thus, due to wide band gap combined with hole selectivity characteristics, GQDs can contribute to enhance the light absorption in the active layer/material of the solar cells and at the same time improve the hole extraction efficiency. Notably, by engineering the functional groups, the band gap, light transmission/absorption and electrical properties of GQDs can be tailored over a wide range to meet the performance-specific properties. Indeed, the application of GQDs has improved the performance of organic photovoltaics (OPVs) [21], dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) [22], perovskite solar cells (PSCs) [23], silicon solar cells (SSCs) [24] and other novel heterojunction solar cells [25].

Li et al. [224] explored GQDs as a hole transport layer in OPVs. The GQDs were synthesized using carbon fiber precursor following acid treatment approach, and then spin-coated onto the glass/ITO substrate, leading to the development of the following device structure: glass/ITO/GQDs/active layer/LiF/Al. To uncover the potential of GQDs, the cells with GO as hole transport layer were also studied in the following cell configuration: glass/ITO/GO/active layer/LiF/Al. Two types of active layers were explored namely 1) P3HT:PC61BM (called polymeric solar cells, PSCs) and 2) small molecule DR3TBDT as the donor and PC71BM as the acceptor (called small-molecule solar cells, SMSCs). They realized efficiencies of 3.51% and 6.82% in GQDs-based PSCs and SMSCs which were far better than when these cells were made using GO as a hole transport layer. Wu et al. [21] developed inverted bulk heterojunction OPVs with a configuration of ITO/ZnO/active layer/MoO3/Ag. They used different active layer such as poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT), P3HT: (6,6)-phenyl-C61 butyric acid methylester (PCBM), P3HT:GQDs and P3HT:PCBM:GQDs. The GQDs were synthesized by a photo-Fenton reaction on GO. With P3HT as the active layer, the solar cell efficiency was reported to be about 0.001% which was increased to a maximum of about 0.25% when 1 wt% GQDs were introduced to P3HT. Likewise, the fabricated cell with P3HT:PCBM active layer showed an efficiency of about 2.96% which was considerably increased to about 4.13% when 1 wt% GQDs were added to P3HT:PCBM. This reflects the role of GQDs in enhancing the power conversion efficiency of OPVs. The energy band diagram of OPVs with GQDs inactive layers is shown in Fig. 19 a and b. The improved property due to the introduction of GQDs was attributed to enhanced excitation separation and the formation of an excellent carrier transport channel. Xu et al. [225] proposed the application of GQDs as thickness-insensitive cathode interlayer (CIL). In particular, GQDs functionalized ammonium iodide (GQD:NI) was employed to construct OPV with the following device configuration: ITO/PEDOT:PSS/PCDTBC:PC71BM/GQD:NI/Al. The solar cell device with GQD:NI as CIL exhibited excellent power conversion efficiency of 7.49% which was considerably higher than the cell (5.38%) without CIL. Importantly, the cell performance was found to be insensitive to the thickness of GQD:NI based CIL in the thickness range of 2–22 nm.

Lately, GQDs have shown the potential to tailor the performance of perovskite solar cells. Zhang et al. [23] introduced GQDs in perovskite film to construct organic-inorganic perovskite solar cells. The application of GQDs improved the quality of perovskite film and enhances the charge carrier extraction efficiency. Pang et al. [226] employed SnO2 as an electron transport layer in PSCs. With SnO2 as an electron transport layer, the PSC shows a power conversion efficiency of 18.6%. They also decorated the SnO2 layer with GQDs and the resultant PSC shows higher power conversion efficiency of 21.1%. This suggests that the application of GQDs also improves the performance of the conventional electron transport layer. Fang et al. [227] employed GQDs on perovskite film to passivate thing dangling bonds at the grain boundaries and enhancing the electron-transport property. Due to these reasons, the organic-inorganic halide PSC with GQDs shows higher efficiency than the cell without GQDs.

GQDs have also attracted huge attention for DSSCs where TiO2 is mainly used as an inorganic substance. Fang et al. [228] applied GQDs on TiO2 photo anode and studied the electrical response of resultant DSSCs. The cell without GQDs containing photo electrodes showed a power conversion efficiency of 5.1% which was considerably increased to a maximum value of 6.1% for the cell with GQDs containing photo electrode. The improved photovoltaic performance of GQDs containing DSSC was attributed to the excellent photo response of the GQDs and injection of hot electrons from GQDs to TiO2. Prabhagar M. et al. explored boron-doped GQDs (B-GQDs) for DSSCs, Fig. 20 a [22]. They prepared a novel multi-constituent photo anode based on B-GQDs-ZnO nanocomposite for DSSC. The resultant DSSC with B-GQDs-ZnO nanocomposite revealed a photo conversion efficiency of 3.7% which was considerably higher than the DSSC with deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA)-ZnO nanoflower photoelectrode (2.1%) i.e. photoelectrode without GQDs as one of the constituents.

GQDs have also been employed to enhance the performance of a variety of heterojunction solar cells. Silicon heterojunction (SHJ) solar cells reveal very high efficiency and are widely used for commercial applications. Application GQDs are expected to further improve the performance of silicon solar cells due to their unique photo response/photo management and carrier selective characteristics. Tsai et al. [24] explored GQDs to tailor the performance of bifacial silicon heterojunction solar cell with intrinsic layer. The device was made on n-type wafer with the following structure: Ag grids/ITO/p-a-Si:H/i-a-Si:H/n-type wafer/i-a-Si:H/n-a-Si:H/ITO/Ag full contact, and finally the GQDs were applied on top of the solar cells. The SHJ solar cell with GQDs showed the highest conversion efficiency of 16.55% at GQDs concentration of 0.3%. The control SHJ solar cell device without GQDs at the front revealed a conversion efficiency of 14.77%, suggesting that GQDs play a vital role in the performance enhancement of SHJ solar cells. The improved efficiency of SHJ solar cell with GQDs was mainly attributed to the GQDs mediated down conversion/photo management in the device. Diao et al. [230] explored the use of GQDs as p-type contact layer in GQDs/n-Si heterojunction solar cells. With graphene transparent electrodes, the authors realized power conversion efficiency as high as 12.35%, which is remarkable. The application of GQDs in GQDs/n-Si heterojunction solar cells allowed the ease conduction of holes and blocked the electrons which ultimately maximized the power conversion efficiency. Tsai et al. [229] developed high-performance organic/inorganic hybrid solar cells based on poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS)/Si. The engineering of this configuration by the addition of GQDs aids in enhancing the performance of organic-inorganic hybrid solar cells, Fig. 20b. It was shown that the PEDOT:PSS/Si hybrid cell displays a short circuit current density of 32.11 mA/cm2 which was considerably increased to 36.26 mA/cm2 by the addition of GQDs in PEDOT:PSS. Likewise the photo conversion efficiency of PEDOT:PSS/Si hybrid cell was found to be 11.5% which was substantially increased to 13.22% by the application of GQDs in PEDOT:PSS. The GQDs driven enhanced properties in the organic-inorganic hybrid cell were attributed to excellent light management i.e. optical properties. Thus, light management in terms of absorption/transmission and specific carrier transport/carrier blockage are the major features of GQDs for enhancing the performance of silicon-based heterojunction solar cells. Shin et al. [231] adopted similar approach and developed GQDs incorporated PEDOT:PSSS-GQDs/porous Si heterojunction solar cells. The best cell yielded a high-power conversion efficiency of 10.49%.

Next, apart from SHJ solar cells, GQDs have also been explored for another type of heterojunction solar cells. For example, Dutta et al. [25] developed ZNO-GQDs hybrid solar cells where ZnO nanowires were grown on Al-doped ZnO films and subsequently GQDs were applied. The application of GQDs led to the filling up of the spaces between nanowires and ultimately the surface layer turned to be a layer of GQDs. To enhance the carrier transport of hole through the device, they have also applied NN'-diphenyl-N-N′-bis(3-methylphenyl)-1,1′-biphenyl)-4,4′-diamine (TPD) layer. The control device yielded open-circuit voltage of 0.1 V which was substantially increased to 0.8 V when the composite layer was employed, though the cell efficiency was measured to be very low. Khan and Kim [232] used nitrogen functionalized GQDs to boost the performance of Cu (In, Ga)Se2 solar cells. Application of N-GQDs enhanced the down-conversion efficiency which contributed to increase the maximum current output and hence enhances the performance of CIGS solar cell.

Recently, piezoelectric nanogenerator transfers mechanical energy such as various sources, vibration, ultrasonic wave, air/liquid pressures, sound waves to electricity can be utilized for many applications such as energy generation transducers, sensors and actuators. Flexible nanogenerators that scavenge various types of mechanical energy exhibit a great potential for powering nanosystems and low-power portable devices. Integration of transparency and flexibility with nanogenerators has recently gained huge interest in realizing the energy harvesting systems in practical life and biomedical application [233–235]. Piezoelectric nanogenerators based on ZnO, P(VDF-TrFE), PbZrTiO3 and BaTiO3 nanostructures are very promising, as they offer the possibility of supplying power for the future generation of self-powered wireless electronic devices [236–238]. In particular, semiconducting piezoelectric materials show a great advantage over insulating piezoelectric materials in many fields, such as piezoelectric diodes, piezoelectric field-effect transistors, piezophotonics, and piezophototronics application, due to their attractive coexisting dual properties [238–240]. Several attempts to fabricate transparent and flexible nanogenerators have been applied and carbon-based materials such as graphene, carbon nanotubes and GQDs are used as fillers or as conducting substrates [241,242]. Apart from the piezoelectric nanogenerators, recently, a new type of mechanical energy harvesting device, called triboelectric nanogenerator based on triboelectric and electrostatic effects, has been reported. In the triboelectric nanogenerator, electric energy conversion is realized by the contact/rubbing between two materials that differ in the polarity of triboelectricity [243–245]. Various types of triboelectric nanogenerators based on metal-polymer, metal-metal in single and double electrode configuration are reported. To enhance the contact surface area and energy conversion process, numerous nanoparticles are decorated on the polymer or metal surface in nanogenerators. Moreover, GQDs, an anisotropic 0D nanostructured carbon materials having one or a few layers of graphene with chemical groups connected on the edges have become the most popular materials due to their outstanding properties such as large surface area, low cytotoxicity semiconducting/conducting and fluorescent properties with excellent biocompatibility [245,246]. Recently, Chong Lu et al., have reported a facile fabrication approach to fabricate piezoelectric nanogenerator based on the GQDs and poly(vinylidene fluoride) [247]. A self-powered piezoelectric polymer matrix composite that can efficiently convert mechanical, vibrational and hydraulic energy into electricity without any treatment of electrical poling is achieved by GQDs. They have reported that hybrid composite, based on poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF) with a luminescent GQDs, finalized its self-polarization process by high-pressure crystallization and based on these configuration simple piezoelectric generators was fabricated.

The GQDs based piezoelectric nanogenerators show an open-circuit voltage of 0.8 V and short-circuit current of 25 nA. The surface morphology SEM image of the etched surface of the GQD/PVDF sample is studied, in which the fern-branch 3D hierarchical polymeric structures, with individual, featured β phase macro/nanowires extending in various directions, were revealed by them (see Fig. 21 ). They have reported that by increasing the GQD loadings in GQD/PVDF composites, the micro/nanowire assemblies were crystallized. Finally, the piezoelectric nanogenerator was fabricated and the output performance was shown in Fig. 21 d,e. An open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current of the piezoelectric generators based on various GQD/PVDF composites was measured. The results that both the voltage and current increased almost linearly with the increase of GQD loadings, evidently demonstrating the excellent tuning ability of GQDs for the electric output of GQD/PVDF composites. Particularly, the open-circuit voltage of 0.75 V and short-circuit current of 25.7 nA of a GQD/PVDF sample with 3 wt% GQD loading were achieved, which were more than four times larger than those of the pure PV.

In another work, Zhongwei Xu and his team reported the triboelectric electronic-skin based on GQDs for application in self-powered, smart, artificial fingers [248]. They have designed micro-gaps and make the e-skin respond sensitively to various mechanical stimulations, including pressing, stretching, folding, and twisting. To achieve it, Ag nanowires coated with GQDs s are employed as the electrode for triboelectric nanogenerator, as well as the friction layer, to increase the sensitivity to external mechanical stimulations. The basic structure and design of the e-skin based on the GQDs-coated Ag NW are shown in Fig. 22 . Pattern PDMS layer was used as a supporting matrix. SEM images of the Ag NWs and Low-magnification TEM image of G-Ag NW and GQDs are shown in Fig. 22. Transparent and lightweight e-skin exhibits different mechanical deformations, including twisting and stretching. They reported that ultra-lightweight with an areal density of approximately 130 g/m2, which makes it suitable for applications in portable and wearable electronics.

They have fabricated triboelectric nanogenerators with GQDs and without GQDs and output performance was compared. To enhance the electron transfer process between the Ag nanowires and PDMS, graphene QDs are used. Triboelectric nanogenerator performance (short-circuit currents) of a graphene QD solution with a concentration of 10 mg/mL is blade coated on the Ag NW network is measured. Fig. 23 showed the output currents from the e-skin with graphene QDs are enhanced dramatically. An output short-circuits current density of ∼10 mA/cm2 is produced, which is approximately 20 times larger than that of the device without graphene QDs under a pressure of 10 N from the e-skin. They have investigated that output performance was improved under the increase of load and introducing the GQDs. The, increased pressure leads to increased current output because the triboelectric charge density increases when the contact surface area is increased through GQDs.

Supercapacitors (SCs) are drawing attention because of their high-power density, short charge-discharge timing, and long cycle life, because of various charge storage systems, SCs are typically split into electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) and pseudocapacitors by their diverse charge holding mechanisms. Electrodeposited GQDs on gold finger electrodes are published by Liu and co-workers [249]. As presented in Fig. 24 a, the electrophoretic deposition of GQDs with interdigital finger electrodes was achieved for the preparation of GQDs/GQDs symmetric supercapacitor (MSC). The MSC made 32 in-plane interdigital gold micro-electrodes i.e., 16 positive and negative each. The micro-electrode has a dimension of 230 μm × 10 mm (width x length). The gap between adjacent micro-electrodes was 0.2 mm. The designed MSC shows a 534.7 mF cm−2 of capacitance with 0.074 μWhcm−2 of energy density and 7.5 μWcm−2 of power density at 15 μAcm−2. The rate performance recorded was up to 1000 Vs−1 (Fig. 24b), and capacitance decay of 6% only compared to its initial capacitance after 5000 cycles. GQD-doped polyaniline (GQD-P) composites have been investigated by Mondal et al. [250] and prepared to employ chemical aniline oxidation. Preparing high-performance supercapacitor electrodes, a GQD-P composite preparation method is shown in Fig. 24c, to exploit the possible alliance of GQD and PANI. Synthesized GQD composites show ∼1044 Fg-1 of capacitance at 1 Ag-1. The strong electrochemical capacitive behavior of the GQD-P composites is shown in Fig. 24d, which was investigated by the GCD. All of the GCD curves gave a triangular shape with good reversibility of GQD-P material during the GCD process. On the other hand, an excellent cyclic stability was found with 80.1% capacity retention after 3000 cycles.

Jia et al. reported heterostructure of GQD/MnO2 nanosheets to achieve high potential window and used as efficient supercapacitors, a mechanism is shown in Fig. 25 a [251]. A GQD/MnO2 heterostructure electrode was designed in-situ to boost the potential window up to 1.3 V for efficient aqueous supercapacitors. The specific capacitance obtained was 1170 Fg-1 at 5 mV s−1 (Fig. 25b) and capacity retention observed was 92.7% after 10,000 cycles. Additionally, a 2.3 V aqueous asymmetric supercapacitor is assembled using GQD/MnO2-3//nitrogen-doped graphene, it shows an excellent energy density with good power densities of 118 Wh kg−1 and 923 W kg−1. Zhang et al. described a single-step hydrothermal route for the synthesis of MnCo2O4.5 and GQDs having various morphologies, where GQDs served as conductive filling agents and structure triggering agents [252]. Among different synthesized morphologies, the composite of nanoneedle synthesized using GQDs (SEM image MnCo2O4.5 -40 GQDs shown in Fig. 25c) shows the best capacitive output of 1625 Fg-1 at 1 Ag-1 (Fig. 25d), on contrary, 4 times less capacitance value was demonstrated by pure MnCo2O4.5 nanospheres.

The supercapacitive performance of activated carbon (AC) can be boosted by constructing a conductive network using GQDs over activated carbon. Generally, high surface area AC has been explored extensively for supercapacitors but they are limited due to the low conductivity and stagnant electrochemical processes resulting from microporous structures. The GQDs decorated AC possessing a specific surface area (2829 m2g-1), delivered a gravimetric capacitance of 388 Fg-1 at 1 Ag-1 with desirable rate performance [253]. Wang et al. reported NiCo2S4/tryptophan-functionalized GQDs nanohybrid (NiCo2S4/Trp-GQDs), which was synthesized by a hydrothermal route [254]. The designed supercapacitor electrode provides a high gravimetric capacitance of 1453.1 Fg-1 at 1 Ag-1 in a 3 M KOH electrolyte. Additionally, NiCO2S4/GQDs/AC with asymmetric configuration was designed which exhibited 157.1 Wh kg−1 of energy density at 800 W kg−1 of power density.

A stable connection is required in Lithium-ion battery (LIB) electrodes between a Faradaic metal oxide (charge storage) and a conductive material (carbon-based materials). A performance limiting factor is also the charge transfer inside the metal oxide in batteries. The coupling of charge transfer components with nanoscale charge storage is a promising idea to overcome this drawback; this will optimize the interfacial contact area and enhance the charging/discharging action. Chao et al. reported GQD coated VO2 arrays for LIB and sodium-ion battery (NIB) (shown in Fig. 26 ) as highly durable electrodes [255]. Graphene foam braced GQD-anchored VO2 electrodes provide a capacity of 420 mAh g−1 with excellent capacity retention (∼94%) after 1500 cycles. Tam et al. reported a hydrothermal approach to preparing new carbon-based composites of B-doped GQD on a graphene hydrogel (GH-BGQD) [256]. A flexible Zn–air battery was fabricated by GH-BGQD air electrode attains a gravimetric capacity of 687 mAh g−1, and 112 m W cm−2 of a high-power density.

In this review article, we have comprehensively summarized the multiple ways to boost the research and development in GQDs synthesis followed by their properties and advancement in applications including biosensors, bioimaging, drug delivery, antimicrobial, phototherapy, photocatalysis, fuel cell, solar cell, energy harvesting and energy storage. Recent development in GQDs synthesis by various top-down and bottom approaches is discussed. Further, we have also discussed GQDs interesting properties such as absorption, PL and electrical properties, which make them potential candidates for many potential applications. GQDs especially colloidal semiconductor GQDs, possess properties including high quantum yields, narrow fluorescence spectra, broad absorption and excellent photostability, making them extremely powerful in bioimaging and sensing applications. Moreover, a high surface-to-volume ratio further renders GQDs ideal for functionalization to obtain good biocompatibility and sensitivity to the environment. The unique properties of GQDs like high stability, high solubility, enhanced surface area, good conductivity along non-toxicity make GQDs a promising material for photocatalysis as well as for electrocatalysis applications. In addition, the tunable band gap of GQDs makes them promising materials for fuel cell and solar cells ranging from OPVs, DSSCs, PSCs and SCs have been critically reviewed. Further, we have reviewed the GQDs based transparent and flexible materials for their potential applications in powering portable electronics. In the last section, we have discussed the progress of GQDs applications in supercapacitors development.

Despite various established synthesis methods and their potential applications, GQDs synthesis is still challenging. Future research on GQDs-based materials may improve their versatile applications as follows;
1.
First of all, there is a lack of consistent definitions of GQDs in literature, which should be addressed properly. In addition, GQDs synthesis on large scale has not been achieved so far, which needs to be established for commercial applications. Not only scale production but scalable purification also should be considered.
2.
GQDs are being synthesized by using the various approach, which produces large heterogeneity in size, shape and their properties. Thus, we need to fully characterize and understand their fluorescence and other properties very precisely for a particular application.
3.
For biosensor and bioimaging applications, QGDs fluorescence intensity and quantum yield are key parameters for their commercialization. However, GQDs have a relatively low PL intensity and quantum yield than semiconductor QDs and organic dyes. GQDs quantum yield may be improved by heteroatom doping and surface passivation.
4.
GQDs functionalization and dispersion stability need to be improved for biomedical applications. For drug delivery applications, a large quantity of GQDs is not needed but their controlled size and purity need to be taken into account.
5.
Controlled size and shaped QGDs shall be developed for bandgap tunability, which is required for solar cells and energy-related applications.

Although GQDs has shown remarkable potential for a wide range of applications. We hope that further research on the development of scalable and controlled synthesis methods along with the understanding of their properties will improve the chance of commercialization of GQDs-based materials for potential applications.
